proposals.title: Establishing Defined Syntrophic Co-cultures of Anaerobic Methane-Oxidizing (AOM) Consortia to Elucidate Metabolic Interactions and Rates
proposals.motivation: Anaerobic oxidation of methane (AOM) coupled to sulfate reduction is a key biogeochemical process in marine sediments, regulating methane flux and the deep carbon cycle. Despite its importance, the specific metabolic interactions and exchange mechanisms between anaerobic methanotrophic (ANME) archaea and their sulfate-reducing bacterial (SRB) partners remain poorly understood due to the lack of pure or defined co-cultures. Cultivation is essential to move beyond genomic inferences and measure the fundamental physiological constraints and rates governing this syntrophy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific ANME-SRB consortia can be isolated and maintained in stable laboratory co-cultures by recreating their native sediment physicochemical gradients, and that these cultures will reveal novel metabolic interdependencies and allow for the first direct measurements of cell-specific AOM rates under controlled conditions.
proposals.method: We will employ high-pressure, continuous-flow bioreactors and gradient gel-stabilized media systems to mimic the in situ conditions of deep-sea sediments. Inocula will be sourced from well-characterized AOM-active sediments (e.g., Hydrate Ridge). Enrichments will be monitored via methane/sulfate consumption, 16S rRNA gene sequencing, and fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH). Subsequent isolation attempts will use targeted dilution-to-extinction series and manipulation of electron carriers (e.g., zero-valent iron, humics) or potential direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET) facilitators. Metabolic rates will be quantified using radiotracer (14C-CH4, 35S-SO42-) and stable isotope (13C-CH4) techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first laboratory model systems for AOM, enabling definitive experiments on syntrophic mechanism, energy conservation, and growth requirements. It will yield foundational data on the intrinsic metabolic rates and thermodynamic limits of AOM consortia, directly informing global models of subsurface carbon reactivity and microbial survival under energy limitation.
proposals.novelty: While ANME-SRB consortia have been enriched, obtaining defined, transferable co-cultures has been a long-standing, unresolved challenge. Our approach uniquely combines high-pressure cultivation with gradient-based systems and targeted manipulation of electron transfer pathways to overcome previous cultivation bottlenecks.

proposals.title: Quantifying Electron Transfer and Metabolic Coupling in Anaerobic Methane-Oxidizing Consortia via Combined Rate Measurements and Isotopic Probing
proposals.motivation: Anaerobic oxidation of methane (AOM) is a globally significant biogeochemical process that regulates methane flux from marine sediments, mediated by syntrophic consortia of anaerobic methanotrophic archaea (ANME) and sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB). Despite its importance, the exact biochemical pathway and the nature of direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET) versus diffusive metabolite transfer remain unresolved. Elucidating this is critical for understanding the fundamental controls on methane reactivity and carbon cycling in subsurface sediments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AOM in ANME/SRB consortia is driven by a direct, multi-step electron transfer mechanism from ANME to partner bacteria, with the rate of electron flow being the primary constraint on the overall metabolic rate, rather than the diffusion of chemical intermediates.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of laboratory sediment slurry incubations with deep-sea methane seep sediments and highly enriched consortia. Using 13C-labeled methane and 35S-sulfate, we will perform parallel, time-resolved measurements of methane oxidation and sulfate reduction rates at varying temperature and pressure regimes to establish kinetic coupling. This will be integrated with targeted inhibition experiments (e.g., using specific cytochrome inhibitors) and nanoscale secondary ion mass spectrometry (NanoSIMS) to map single-cell anabolic activity and quantify carbon assimilation in both partners, directly linking electron donor oxidation to electron acceptor reduction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitatively constrained model of electron flow in AOM consortia, directly testing the DIET hypothesis. It will define the metabolic rate and its controls under in-situ conditions, contributing a fundamental piece to models of sub-seafloor carbon and sulfur cycling. The results will refine our understanding of energy conservation in the deep biosphere's slowest metabolisms.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have described the organisms and genes involved, our approach uniquely combines precise, simultaneous rate measurements of both methane and sulfate transformations with single-cell isotopic imaging to directly link the activity of the archaeal and bacterial partners, moving beyond correlation to mechanistic causation.

proposals.title: Geochemical triggers for syntrophic aggregate formation in marine sediments: Linking iron-sulfur-organic matter dynamics to microbial symbiosis
proposals.motivation: Recent studies, including those by Jørgensen and colleagues, reveal that deeply buried marine sediments harbor a metabolically active, slow-growing biosphere where syntrophic interactions are likely essential for survival. The specific environmental conditions that initiate and sustain the physical structuring of these microbial consortia into aggregates remain unknown, creating a critical gap in understanding the fundamental organization and energy flow of the deep subsurface ecosystem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the formation of syntrophic aggregates in anoxic sediments is triggered by a specific geochemical threshold, where the reactivity of organic carbon and the availability of soluble iron(II) and sulfate create a thermodynamic landscape that favors direct interspecies electron transfer over diffusive metabolite exchange.
proposals.method: We will conduct laboratory incubation experiments with sediment slurries from Aarhus Bay, manipulating key variables: organic carbon reactivity (using defined substrates like acetate and complex macromolecules), sulfate concentration, and Fe(II) availability. Rates of sulfate reduction, iron reduction, and methanogenesis will be quantified using radiotracer (35S-SO4) and gas chromatography. Aggregate formation will be monitored via catalyzed reporter deposition-fluorescence in situ hybridization (CARD-FISH) and scanning electron microscopy. A reaction-transport model will be developed to integrate rate measurements and geochemical profiles, identifying the critical parameter space (e.g., carbon oxidation rate / electron acceptor ratio) that predicts aggregate initiation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a predictive, geochemical framework for when and where structured microbial symbioses form in subsurface sediments. It will directly link the metabolic rates of the biosphere to its physical organization, providing a mechanistic understanding of energy conservation in the deep biosphere that refines global carbon models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described aggregate existence and metabolic rates separately, this study uniquely seeks to identify the *triggering conditions* by integrating controlled geochemical manipulation with direct visualization and thermodynamic modeling of aggregate formation.

proposals.title: Microscale Thermodynamic Constraints on Anaerobic Methane Oxidation: Energy Yields and Kinetics in Single Syntrophic Aggregates
proposals.motivation: Anaerobic oxidation of methane (AOM) coupled to sulfate reduction is a key biogeochemical process in marine sediments, regulating methane flux and supporting a deep biosphere. However, bulk rate measurements and thermodynamic models for dispersed communities cannot resolve the energetic constraints and kinetic limitations within the physical microenvironments where syntrophic consortia of anaerobic methanotrophs (ANME) and sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) actually interact. Understanding the energy landscape at the scale of a single aggregate is critical for predicting microbial survival and process rates under the extreme energy limitation of the deep subsurface.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the thermodynamic energy yield for AOM within a single aggregate is significantly lower than bulk sediment calculations predict, and that diffusion-limited substrate transfer and direct interspecies electron transfer create kinetic bottlenecks that control the observed metabolic rate.
proposals.method: We will use microsensor technology (H2S, CH4, pH) combined with stable isotope probing (13C-CH4) and nanoscale secondary ion mass spectrometry (NanoSIMS) on individually extracted aggregates from Aarhus Bay or Hydrate Ridge sediments. A custom microfluidic flow cell will allow precise control of CH4 and SO42- gradients while monitoring consumption rates via time-resolved microsensor measurements. Thermodynamic yields (ΔG) will be calculated in situ using measured aggregate-scale concentrations. Kinetic parameters will be derived by modeling reaction-diffusion processes within the aggregate architecture, visualized via FISH-CLSM.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct measurement of per-aggregate energy yields for AOM, fundamentally linking microbial thermodynamics to observed cell-specific metabolic rates. It will establish a quantitative framework for scaling from single aggregates to sediment-scale process rates, refining models of carbon cycling and microbial life under energy limitation in the deep biosphere.
proposals.novelty: While bulk AOM rates are well-studied, quantifying the thermodynamics and kinetics within the physically constrained, syntrophic unit where the reaction occurs is a novel and necessary scale-down. This approach moves beyond community-level averages to probe the mechanistic drivers of metabolic cooperation and survival.

proposals.title: Decoupling Pressure and Temperature Effects: Quantifying the Impact of In Situ Hydrostatic Pressure on Sulfate Reduction Rates in Deep Marine Sediments
proposals.motivation: Current estimates of microbial sulfate reduction (SR) in the deep biosphere are largely based on laboratory incubations performed at in situ temperature but at atmospheric pressure. This neglects the significant hydrostatic pressure of deep-sea and sub-seafloor environments, which can exceed 100 bar. Given that pressure is known to affect microbial physiology and enzyme kinetics, ignoring this variable introduces a major uncertainty in global models of carbon oxidation and sulfur cycling, such as those quantifying the metabolic rate of the biosphere.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the application of in situ hydrostatic pressure will significantly increase measured sulfate reduction rates in deep-sea sediment incubations compared to atmospheric-pressure controls at the same temperature, and that this pressure effect is modulated by sediment depth and the associated organic matter reactivity.
proposals.method: We will conduct shipboard and laboratory experiments using sediment cores from contrasting deep-sea sites (e.g., Peru Margin, Aarhus Bay). Using pre-2026 high-pressure incubation systems (e.g., stainless steel reactors or pressure vessels with butyl rubber stoppers), we will incubate sediment slurries and intact cores under a matrix of conditions: combining in situ temperature with either (1) in situ hydrostatic pressure or (2) atmospheric pressure. Sulfate reduction rates (SRR) will be quantified using the 35S-sulfate radiotracer core injection method, with parallel measurements of dissolved iron species and porewater sulfate to assess the interplay with iron reduction. Cell-specific SRR will be determined via catalyzed reporter deposition-fluorescence in situ hybridization (CARD-FISH) combined with microautoradiography (MICRO-CARD-FISH).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic quantification of the pressure effect on sulfate reduction rates across a gradient of deep-sea sedimentary environments. It will deliver pressure-correction factors to refine global estimates of anaerobic carbon oxidation in the seabed. Furthermore, it will elucidate whether the deep, hot subseafloor biosphere sustains higher metabolic rates than previously inferred from low-pressure experiments, directly testing paradigms of microbial survival under energy limitation.
proposals.novelty: While the individual effects of temperature and pressure on microbial activity have been studied, their synergistic effect on sulfate reduction—the dominant anaerobic carbon oxidation pathway in marine sediments—has not been systematically decoupled and quantified. This moves beyond the standard practice of constant-pressure incubations to explicitly test a key environmental variable that is routinely omitted.

proposals.title: Geochemical controls on the temperature optimum of subseafloor sulfate-reducing communities
proposals.motivation: The deep subseafloor biosphere is a vast, energy-limited habitat where microbial communities persist over geological timescales. Pre-2026 studies, including those by Jørgensen, show that these communities exhibit distinct temperature optima for key processes like sulfate reduction, but the underlying controls remain unclear. Understanding whether these optima are shaped by intrinsic physiological adaptation or by extrinsic geochemical factors, such as organic matter reactivity and electron acceptor availability, is crucial for predicting microbial activity and carbon cycling across diverse sedimentary environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the community temperature optimum for sulfate reduction in marine sediments is not a fixed physiological trait but is dynamically influenced by the quality of organic matter and the in-situ sulfate concentration, with higher-quality substrates and greater sulfate availability enabling activity at higher temperatures.
proposals.method: We will conduct laboratory incubations of sediment cores from a thermal gradient (e.g., Aarhus Bay, Baltic Sea, and a warmer site like the Guaymas Basin) at multiple temperatures (0-80°C). For each temperature and site, we will quantify potential sulfate reduction rates (SRR) using 35-S radiotracer assays under manipulated conditions: (1) with native organic matter, (2) with amendments of labile organic substrates (e.g., acetate, lactate), and (3) under varying sulfate concentrations. The temperature optimum will be determined from the resulting SRR vs. temperature curves. Geochemical parameters (porewater sulfate, iron speciation, TOC, and organic matter degradation state via Δ14C and C/N ratios) will be characterized to correlate with the observed optima.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link microbial metabolic thermodynamics to sedimentary geochemistry, providing a mechanistic framework for why temperature optima vary across depositional environments. It will improve models of subsurface carbon oxidation by incorporating geochemical controls on thermal adaptation, offering new insights into the survival strategies of the deep biosphere under energy limitation.
proposals.novelty: While temperature effects on microbial rates are well-studied, explicitly testing how the quality of energy sources (organic matter) and electron acceptor availability modulates the community-level temperature optimum for a key biogeochemical process in natural sediments is a novel and integrative approach.

proposals.title: Temperature-dependent coupling of specific organic carbon oxidation pathways to sulfate reduction in marine sediments revealed by dual 13C/35S radiotracer incubation
proposals.motivation: The deep subseafloor biosphere is a vast reservoir of microbial life, but the metabolic strategies enabling survival under energy limitation and high temperature are poorly constrained. Pre-2026 studies show sulfate reduction persists at temperatures up to ~80°C, yet the specific organic carbon substrates fueling this process and how their utilization shifts with temperature remain unknown, limiting our understanding of deep biosphere carbon cycling and energy fluxes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the temperature response of sulfate reduction in marine sediments is governed by a shift in the dominant organic carbon oxidation pathways, with fermentation of complex polymers coupled to sulfate reduction being more resilient at higher temperatures than direct oxidation of low-molecular-weight compounds.
proposals.method: We will conduct laboratory incubations of sediments from a thermal gradient (e.g., Guaymas Basin) at multiple temperatures (4°C to 80°C). Incubations will use a suite of 13C-labeled substrates (e.g., acetate, amino acids, algal lipids, and complex polymers like chitin) combined with 35S-sulfate to simultaneously trace carbon assimilation into biomass and sulfate reduction rates (SRR). SRR will be quantified via the 35S-radiotracer core injection method. 13C incorporation into microbial lipids (PLFA) and RNA will be analyzed via GC-IRMS and density gradient centrifugation (RNA-SIP), respectively, to identify active taxa and their carbon substrates.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link temperature-dependent sulfate reduction rates to specific carbon metabolisms and microbial identities, quantifying the thermodynamic and microbial controls on deep sediment carbon oxidation. It will provide a mechanistic framework for modeling organic carbon reactivity and sulfate reduction as a function of temperature in the deep biosphere.
proposals.novelty: While 35S-SRR measurements and 13C-SIP are established, their combined application to deconvolute temperature effects on specific carbon oxidation pathways supporting sulfate reduction in sediments is novel, moving beyond bulk rate measurements to a process-specific understanding.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Past Thermal Regimes in Marine Sediments Using the Genomic Fingerprints of Sulfate-Reducing Prokaryotes
proposals.motivation: Temperature is a primary control on microbial metabolism and survival in the deep biosphere, yet past thermal histories of sediments are poorly constrained, limiting our understanding of long-term carbon cycling and microbial evolution. Current paleothermometers (e.g., lipid biomarkers, vitrinite reflectance) are not directly linked to the metabolic guilds driving key biogeochemical processes like sulfate reduction. This project aims to develop a novel microbial paleothermometer by leveraging the genetic record of sulfate-reducing prokaryotes (SRPs), a group central to Jørgensen's research on sediment carbon oxidation and sulfur cycling.
proposals.hypothesis: The genomic composition and functional gene adaptations of preserved sulfate-reducing communities in marine sediments encode a quantifiable signature of the thermal regimes they experienced, allowing past in situ temperatures to be inferred from DNA sequences recovered from drill cores.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and new metagenomic datasets from sediment cores with known thermal histories (e.g., IODP expeditions). For SRPs identified via dsrAB genes, we will extract features including: 1) taxonomic composition weighted by thermal preference niches from culture collections; 2) amino acid composition and predicted stability of key metabolic proteins (e.g., APS reductase, cytochrome c3); and 3) codon adaptation indices correlated with optimal growth temperature. A machine learning model (e.g., a Random Forest or Gradient Boosting regressor) will be trained on these genomic features against known temperature profiles to establish a predictive framework. The model will be validated on independent cores from contrasting thermal settings.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-of-its-kind genomic tool for reconstructing thermal history in subsurface environments, directly linking biogeochemistry to paleoenvironmental conditions. It will provide new constraints on the thermal limits and adaptation of the deep subseafloor biosphere, refining models of organic carbon reactivity and sulfate reduction over geological timescales.
proposals.novelty: While microbial community composition is known to correlate with temperature, the inverse problem—deducing a quantitative thermal history from preserved genetic signatures—is unexplored. This approach moves beyond correlation to develop a predictive, mechanistic model rooted in the physiological constraints of a key functional guild.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Temperature Response and Energetic Limits of Microbial Sulfide Oxidation in Marine Sediments
proposals.motivation: The oxidation of sedimentary sulfide is a critical biogeochemical process, closing the sulfur cycle and influencing iron and carbon cycling. While sulfate reduction's temperature response has been studied extensively, the temperature characteristics and metabolic constraints of the opposing sulfide oxidation process in sediments remain poorly quantified. Understanding this is key to predicting how benthic sulfur cycling and its associated microbial communities respond to environmental temperature changes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the temperature response of sedimentary sulfide oxidation follows a distinct Arrhenius relationship, with a lower apparent activation energy than sulfate reduction, and that its rate becomes increasingly limited by the low energy yield of the reaction at in situ temperatures.
proposals.method: We will apply the established thermal gradient incubation method, analogous to recent studies on sulfate reduction, to intact sediment cores from Aarhus Bay. Cores will be incubated in a continuous-flow system with a stable thermal gradient (0-80°C). We will quantify sulfide oxidation rates using 35S-radiotracer assays (tracking 35SO4 production from 35S-labeled FeS) at multiple temperature points. Concurrently, we will measure porewater chemistry (O2, NO3-, Fe2+, ΣH2S) and use microsensor profiling to resolve the depth and temperature dependence of the oxidation zone. The temperature-rate data will be fitted to the Arrhenius and Macromolecular Rate Theory models to derive activation energies and identify potential thermodynamic constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic quantification of the temperature response function for sedimentary sulfide oxidation, enabling its integration into biogeochemical models. It will directly compare the activation energies of the reduction and oxidation arms of the sedimentary sulfur cycle, testing their symmetry. The results will constrain the energetic limits for chemolithotrophic life in the deep biosphere where sulfide oxidation is a key catabolic strategy.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a sophisticated thermal gradient method, previously used to study sulfate reduction and fermentation, to its counter-process, sulfide oxidation, allowing a direct, controlled comparison of their thermodynamic and kinetic constraints within the same sediment system.

proposals.title: Centennial-scale biomass turnover in deep marine sediments: Quantifying in situ microbial replacement rates via D-amino acid racemization and cell-specific activity measurements
proposals.motivation: The deep subseafloor biosphere is a vast, stable, and energy-limited reservoir of microbial life, but its metabolic and growth dynamics remain poorly constrained on geological timescales. Pre-2026 studies by Jørgensen and others have measured extremely low sulfate reduction and fermentation rates, suggesting cells may persist for millennia, yet direct, in situ measurements of biomass replacement are lacking. Quantifying these turnover rates is crucial for understanding the long-term survival strategies, carbon cycling, and evolutionary trajectories of subsurface life.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in stable, oligotrophic deep sediments, the in situ turnover of microbial biomass occurs on century to millennium timescales, orders of magnitude slower than in surface environments, and is directly coupled to the measured rates of sulfate reduction and fermentation.
proposals.method: We will quantify in situ biomass turnover by analyzing the racemization of D-amino acids in bacterial peptidoglycan extracted from sediment cores from well-characterized sites (e.g., Aarhus Bay, Peru Margin). This geochronological method provides an integrated measure of cellular age. These age estimates will be directly compared with cell-specific metabolic rates obtained via parallel NanoSIMS analyses of stable isotope tracers (13C-acetate, 15N-ammonium) in incubated sediments. A novel rate-constraint model will integrate D/L amino acid ratios, site-specific temperature histories, and measured activity data to calculate biomass replacement rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, geochemically grounded estimates of biomass replacement times in the deep biosphere, moving beyond metabolic rate proxies. It will establish a quantitative link between cellular longevity and specific electron-accepting processes (sulfate, iron reduction). The results will fundamentally refine models of deep biosphere carbon turnover and energy flux.
proposals.novelty: While metabolic rates have been measured, directly dating the biomass itself via D-amino acid racemization in deep sediments is novel. The combined application of this biomarker aging technique with state-of-the-art NanoSIMS-based activity measurements to constrain in situ turnover is a significant methodological advance beyond existing rate studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying the biomass-specific metabolic activity of JS-1, Planctomycetes, and Chloroflexi in methane hydrate-bearing sediments
proposals.motivation: The deep subseafloor biosphere in hydrate sediments is dominated by uncultivated bacterial clades like JS-1, Planctomycetes, and Chloroflexi, yet their specific contributions to carbon cycling remain enigmatic. Bulk rate measurements of sulfate reduction and fermentation cannot resolve the activity of individual groups, creating a critical gap in understanding how the dominant microbial populations sustain the remarkably low metabolic rates characteristic of this environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that JS-1, Planctomycetes, and Chloroflexi exhibit distinct, low biomass-specific metabolic rates for sulfate reduction and fermentation, and that their combined activity accounts for the majority of the catabolic carbon flow in hydrate sediments.
proposals.method: We will integrate quantitative PCR (for 16S rRNA genes and functional genes like dsrAB) and catalyzed reporter deposition-fluorescence in situ hybridization (CARD-FISH) to determine the absolute cellular abundance and biomass of each target group in sediment cores from hydrate-rich sites. These biomass estimates will be coupled with nanoscale secondary ion mass spectrometry (nanoSIMS) analyses of sediment-slurries incubated with stable isotope-labeled substrates (e.g., 13C-acetate, 15N-ammonium) to measure group-specific substrate incorporation rates. Finally, we will calculate biomass-normalized activity by correlating cell counts with bulk rate measurements of sulfate reduction (35S-radiotracer) and fermentation products.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, taxon-resolved link between standing biomass and process rates in the deep hydrate biosphere. It will constrain the metabolic efficiency and ecological roles of the dominant uncultured groups, directly testing the paradigm of 'maintenance metabolism' at the population level.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies report bulk rates and relative community composition, this proposal uniquely combines absolute quantification of cellular biomass with direct measurement of substrate uptake at the single-cell/phylogenetic level to deconvolve the catabolic contributions of specific, uncultivated lineages.

proposals.title: Defining the Functional Niche: A Biomimetic Microfluidic Platform to Resolve Metabolic Activity and Community Structure Across a Simulated Methane Hydrate Interface
proposals.motivation: Methane hydrates represent a vast reservoir of carbon and a critical habitat for anaerobic microbial communities that mediate methane and sulfur cycling. However, the physical inaccessibility and destructive nature of core sampling have prevented a causal, mechanistic understanding of how the steep physical-chemical gradients at the hydrate-sediment interface structure the microbial community and its metabolic rates. A non-destructive, controlled model system is needed to link microbial identity, position, and activity.
proposals.hypothesis: The microbial community at the methane hydrate interface is structured into distinct functional zones defined by the intersection of individual metabolic niches with the diffusive gradients of methane, sulfate, and other electron donors/acceptors.
proposals.method: We will construct a biomimetic microfluidic 'gradient chip' with a defined porous matrix (e.g., glass beads or micromodels) to create a stable diffusive boundary layer mimicking a hydrate interface. The system will be inoculated with sediment slurry from known hydrate-impacted sites. We will use in situ chemical imaging (e.g., planar optodes for O2, H2S) and nanoscale secondary ion mass spectrometry (nanoSIMS) following incubation with 13C-CH4 and 34S-SO42- to map active metabolic processes. Spatially resolved transcriptomics (e.g., by laser capture microdissection of defined gradient zones followed by RNA-seq) will link activity to genetic potential.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, spatially explicit maps of metabolic rates (sulfate reduction, anaerobic methane oxidation) and gene expression across a simulated hydrate interface. It will establish a causal framework for how gradient dynamics shape community assembly and function, moving beyond correlative observations from natural cores.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in abandoning destructive sampling of natural hydrates in favor of a controlled, biomimetic platform that allows for the first time the experimental manipulation of gradient parameters and time-resolved, in situ observation of community development based on activity and position.

proposals.title: Quantifying Micron-Scale Chemical Fluxes and Microbial Activity at the Methane Hydrate-Biofilm Interface
proposals.motivation: Methane hydrates represent a vast reservoir of carbon, yet the biogeochemical controls on its stability and microbial oxidation in sediments remain poorly constrained at the relevant physical scale. Pre-2026 work has established the importance of anaerobic oxidation of methane (AOM) coupled to sulfate reduction, but the precise coupling between hydrate dissolution kinetics and microbial metabolic rates at the immediate crystal surface is unknown. This gap limits our ability to predict hydrate-driven carbon cycling and its feedbacks on ocean chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dissolution of single methane hydrate crystals creates steep, dynamic chemical gradients of methane and sulfate over micron-scale distances, which directly control the spatial organization, metabolic rates, and survival strategies of the interfacial biofilm community.
proposals.method: We will use laboratory-grown synthetic methane hydrate crystals incubated with natural sediment inocula under controlled pressure/temperature. Custom-made, glass-encased microsensors (O2, H2S, pH, CH4) with tip diameters <10 µm will be deployed using a motorized micromanipulator to map 2D chemical gradients perpendicular and parallel to the crystal surface. High-resolution profiles will be acquired at defined time intervals. Concurrently, we will use catalyzed reporter deposition-fluorescence in situ hybridization (CARD-FISH) and nanoSIMS on parallel samples to identify and quantify cell-specific anabolic activity of sulfate-reducing bacteria and anaerobic methanotrophs (ANME) within the biofilm.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, spatially resolved measurements of chemical fluxes driving the hydrate-microbe interface. It will yield novel rate constants for hydrate dissolution coupled to microbial consumption and constrain the efficiency of the microbial filter. The data will fundamentally advance mechanistic models of carbon turnover in deep biosphere sediments containing gas hydrates.
proposals.novelty: While microsensors have been used in sediments, their application to map gradients at the surface of a single, dissolving methane hydrate crystal is unprecedented. This approach directly fuses physical hydrate chemistry with microbial ecology at the essential scale of interaction, moving beyond bulk sediment measurements.

proposals.title: Direct quantification of microbial sulfate reduction and iron cycling at the single methane hydrate crystal interface
proposals.motivation: Methane hydrates represent a vast reservoir of carbon in marine sediments, yet the microbial processes that regulate their stability and methane flux at the direct mineral-microbe interface remain unresolved. Pre-2026 studies of sub-seafloor metabolism, including Jørgensen's work on cell-specific sulfate reduction rates, have been constrained to bulk sediment analyses, missing the critical spatial scale where electron donors (methane, organic carbon) and acceptors (sulfate, iron oxides) interact.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specialized, metabolically active microbial consortium, including sulfate-reducing bacteria, exists at the hydrate crystal surface, where it couples the anaerobic oxidation of methane or liberated organic carbon to the reduction of sulfate and reactive iron phases, thereby influencing local hydrate stability.
proposals.method: We will develop a micro-scale incubation system using individual, synthetic methane hydrate crystals. The crystal surface will be exposed to anoxic, sulfate- and Fe(III)-amended seawater under controlled pressure/temperature. Cell-specific activity will be quantified using nanoSIMS combined with stable isotope tracers (13C-DIC, 34S-sulfate, 57Fe). The interfacial microbial community will be characterized via fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) coupled to catalyzed reporter deposition (CARD-FISH) and subsequent 16S rRNA gene sequencing from laser-captured micro-dissected samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct measurements of metabolic rates and identities of microbes at the hydrate interface, fundamentally advancing our mechanistic understanding of the deep biosphere's role in the methane cycle. It will constrain the relative importance of sulfate vs. iron reduction at this critical boundary and inform models of hydrate stability and carbon sequestration in sediments.
proposals.novelty: The proposal moves decisively from bulk sediment rate measurements to the single-crystal scale, a previously inaccessible resolution for hydrate systems, directly testing the spatial coupling of microbial processes to a specific physical substrate.

proposals.title: Integrating dynamic trait-based modules for sulfate reducers and methanogens to project sediment methane fluxes under environmental forcing
proposals.motivation: Current Earth system models treat microbial processes like sulfate reduction and methanogenesis as static functions, creating large uncertainty in projections of methane emissions and carbon sequestration in a warming climate. This is critical for coastal and marine sediments, where the competition between microbial guilds controls methane release. A mechanistic, trait-based approach is needed to predict how microbial functional traits and community shifts modulate biogeochemical rates under perturbation.
proposals.hypothesis: Microbial functional traits for sulfate reduction and methanogenesis, derived from genomic databases and rate measurements, can be dynamically linked to environmental drivers (e.g., temperature, organic matter reactivity, iron availability) to probabilistically project methane production and oxidation fluxes in sediments under scenarios of warming and carbon loading.
proposals.method: We will develop trait-based functional modules by curating genomic traits (e.g., maximum growth rate, substrate affinity, temperature response) for key guilds from pre-2026 databases like METABOLIC and isolate studies. These modules will be integrated into a reactive-transport model framework (e.g., MEDUSA or custom). Process rates will be simulated probabilistically using Monte Carlo methods to propagate trait uncertainty. The model will be calibrated and validated against global time-series from observatories (e.g., IODP, GeoB) and data from cross-scale warming and organic matter addition experiments on sediment cores.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel modeling framework that quantifies uncertainty in methane flux projections from sediments by mechanistically representing microbial ecology. It will deliver globally applicable trait-response functions for sulfate-reducing and methanogenic communities, directly informing IPCC-class models about the stability of the marine carbon sink under climate change.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond static microbial parameters or distribution maps to a dynamic, trait-based probabilistic framework represents a fundamental shift in how microbial processes are represented in global biogeochemical models, explicitly linking genomic potential to ecosystem-scale function under change.

proposals.title: Quantifying Artifacts in Sediment Incubations: A Multi-Tracer Approach to Validate In Situ Sulfate Reduction and Iron Cycling Rates
proposals.motivation: Incubation-based rate measurements are foundational for quantifying microbial metabolism in marine sediments, but the act of sampling, handling, and incubating sediment cores can introduce significant artifacts. These include changes in porewater chemistry, physical disturbance of redox gradients, and decompression, potentially altering the dominant pathways of organic carbon mineralization (e.g., sulfate vs. iron reduction). Resolving these artifacts is critical for accurately scaling measured rates to in situ biogeochemical budgets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that standard slurry and whole-core incubation protocols systematically overestimate sulfate reduction rates and underestimate iron reduction rates due to the artificial exposure of fresh reactive iron phases and the disruption of diffusive substrate supply, thereby misrepresenting the in situ partitioning of terminal electron-accepting processes.
proposals.method: We will conduct parallel experiments on sediments from Aarhus Bay and the Baltic Sea, contrasting (1) in situ measurements using benthic lander deployments with (2) shipboard whole-core incubations and (3) slurry incubations. We will employ a multi-tracer approach, using 35S-sulfate radiotracer, 55Fe-labeled iron oxides, and sensitive measurements of dissolved Fe2+ and sulfide. High-resolution diffusive equilibration in thin films (DET) gels will be deployed in situ and in cores to compare porewater concentration gradients. The specific activity of sulfate-reducing microbes will be assessed via catalyzed reporter deposition-fluorescence in situ hybridization (CARD-FISH) combined with nanoSIMS analysis of 15N-ammonia uptake.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for correcting incubation-induced artifacts in sedimentary rate measurements, directly improving the accuracy of global carbon and sulfur cycle models. It will yield concrete protocols to minimize artifacts, strengthening the empirical basis for studies of the deep subseafloor biosphere and its metabolic constraints.
proposals.novelty: While artifacts are widely acknowledged, this study uniquely integrates in situ lander data with a suite of incubation types and advanced cell-specific activity assays to mechanistically diagnose and quantify the bias, moving beyond qualitative recognition to a predictive, correction-based understanding.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Partitioning of Sedimentary Oxygen Flux Between Organic Carbon Oxidation and the Re-oxidation of Reduced Inorganic Species
proposals.motivation: The benthic oxygen flux is a master variable for marine biogeochemistry, integrating all aerobic respiration in sediments. However, this flux oxidizes both organic carbon and reduced inorganic products (e.g., Fe2+, Mn2+, NH4+, HS-) from anaerobic pathways. The fraction of O2 flux directly coupled to organic carbon mineralization versus the re-oxidation of anaerobic metabolites remains poorly quantified, obscuring our understanding of the true efficiency of carbon burial and the redox cycling of iron and sulfur.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in continental margin sediments, a significant and variable fraction (30-70%) of the total oxygen flux is diverted to re-oxidizing reduced inorganic species, rather than directly oxidizing organic carbon, and that this partitioning is predictable based on sediment reactivity and the depth-integrated rates of anaerobic processes.
proposals.method: We will combine established geochemical techniques in a novel, integrated framework. In intact sediment cores from a gradient of depositional environments, we will simultaneously measure: 1) Total O2 flux via microsensor profiling and core incubations; 2) Depth-integrated rates of sulfate reduction (35S-tracer), iron reduction (Fe2+ production), and methanogenesis (14C); and 3) The production and near-surface re-oxidation of reduced species (Fe2+, Mn2+, ΣH2S, NH4+) using a combination of diffusive gradient in thin films (DGT) probes, microsensors, and porewater analysis. A reaction-transport model will be constrained by these data to explicitly partition the O2 consumption between terminal oxidation of organic carbon and the oxidation of each reduced inorganic product.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, process-based quantification of oxygen flux partitioning in marine sediments. It will yield a fundamental parameter for global carbon cycle models—the efficiency of oxygen use for organic carbon oxidation—and clarify the tightness of coupling between aerobic and anaerobic biogeochemical cycles, particularly the sulfur and iron cycles.
proposals.novelty: While O2 fluxes and anaerobic rates are commonly measured separately, no study has systematically combined high-resolution rate measurements and product analysis to directly apportion the sedimentary O2 sink between its ultimate carbon and inorganic electron donors in a mass-balanced framework.

proposals.title: Quantifying the functional guild structure of subseafloor carbon oxidation: Linking 16S rRNA gene-based community composition to partitioned terminal electron-accepting process rates
proposals.motivation: The metabolic activity of the deep subseafloor biosphere is a major, yet poorly constrained, component of the global carbon cycle. While rate measurements of terminal electron-accepting processes (TEAPs) like sulfate and iron reduction quantify carbon oxidation, they cannot resolve the specific microbial populations responsible, limiting our understanding of community-level metabolic strategies and energy partitioning in this vast habitat.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative abundance of specific 16S rRNA gene-based phylogenetic groups (e.g., Deltaproteobacteria clades, Chloroflexi subgroups, candidate phyla) can serve as quantitative biomarkers to partition total organic carbon oxidation among concurrent TEAPs in marine sediments.
proposals.method: We will integrate established geochemical rate measurements with high-throughput 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing (Illumina MiSeq, targeting V4-V5 region) on depth-resolved sediment cores from contrasting sites (e.g., Aarhus Bay, Baltic Sea). Using a constrained optimization algorithm, we will correlate the quantified rates of sulfate reduction, iron reduction, and methanogenesis with the relative abundances of known functional guilds. This model will be trained on geochemically well-characterized horizons and validated on independent sediment layers to predict carbon oxidation partitioning from community data alone.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, community-based framework for apportioning carbon flow in the subseafloor, moving beyond bulk rate measurements. It will directly link microbial population dynamics to biogeochemical process rates, offering a predictive tool for estimating metabolic activity in sediments where comprehensive rate profiling is impractical.
proposals.novelty: While microbial surveys and process rates are commonly performed in parallel, this study proposes a quantitative, model-driven integration to use community structure as a predictor for partitioned carbon oxidation rates, a step beyond qualitative correlation.

proposals.title: A Global Reassessment of Organic Carbon Oxidation by Iron and Manganese Reduction in Continental Margin Sediments
proposals.motivation: Quantifying the global pathways of organic carbon oxidation in marine sediments is fundamental to understanding the marine carbon cycle and redox budgets. Pre-2026 syntheses have highlighted sulfate reduction as the dominant anaerobic pathway on margins, but recent site-specific studies, including those by Jørgensen and colleagues, suggest metal reduction (especially by reactive iron oxides) may be more significant than previously modeled. A systematic, data-driven upscaling of this revised pathway significance is critically needed to constrain its global magnitude.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the contribution of metal (Fe, Mn) reduction to total anaerobic carbon oxidation in continental margin sediments is at least 25-40%, substantially higher than the <20% estimated in previous global budgets, due to the previously underestimated reactivity and availability of metal oxides.
proposals.method: We will compile a global database of published and unpublished (e.g., from IODP expeditions) rate measurements of sulfate, iron, and manganese reduction, along with solid-phase reactive metal concentrations, from diverse margin settings. Using a spatial GIS framework, we will extrapolate site-specific carbon oxidation rates by each pathway across margin provinces defined by water depth, primary productivity, and sediment type. The extrapolation will employ Monte Carlo simulations to propagate uncertainties in rate measurements and spatial heterogeneity, generating a probabilistic global budget.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first probabilistic, data-constrained global estimate of carbon oxidation via metal reduction in margin sediments, directly challenging and refining existing carbon cycle models. It will establish a new quantitative baseline for the role of sedimentary metal cycling in the ocean's redox state and its coupling to the iron and sulfur cycles.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond the sulfate-centric paradigm by quantitatively integrating recent site-specific evidence for vigorous metal reduction into a robust, uncertainty-aware global spatial model, a synthesis not yet attempted pre-2026.

proposals.title: Electrochemical Probing of Alternative Electron Acceptors for Anaerobic Methane Oxidation in Marine Sediments
proposals.motivation: Anaerobic oxidation of methane (AOM) is a critical biogeochemical process, estimated to consume over 90% of methane produced in marine sediments before it reaches the atmosphere. The canonical pathway, mediated by consortia of anaerobic methanotrophic archaea (ANME) and sulfate-reducing bacteria, is considered the dominant electron sink. However, this sulfate-centric view may overlook other thermodynamically feasible pathways, limiting our understanding of methane's fate in diverse anoxic environments where sulfate is depleted or limited.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the AOM consortium can function as a biogeochemical battery, where methane oxidation (the anodic half-reaction) can be coupled to the reduction of alternative electron acceptors beyond sulfate, such as ferric iron, humic substances, or trace metals, provided their electrochemical potentials are within a viable window.
proposals.method: We will employ electrochemical techniques, specifically cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry, using sediment microelectrodes and bioreactors inoculated with active AOM mats from known sites (e.g., Hydrate Ridge). We will systematically manipulate the electrochemical potential of an electrode, acting as an artificial, non-limiting electron acceptor, to measure current generated from AOM activity. Parallel incubations with natural sediments will quantify AOM rates via 13C-CH4 tracer and pore-water geochemistry in the presence of candidate acceptors (amorphous Fe(III), anthraquinone-2,6-disulfonate as a humic analog, and Mn(IV)), while using specific inhibitors (e.g., molybdate for sulfate reduction) to decouple processes.
proposals.contribution: This work will rigorously test the thermodynamic flexibility of AOM consortia, potentially identifying new electron-accepting niches. It will provide a quantitative, electrochemically grounded framework for understanding energy constraints in the deep biosphere. The findings could explain methane oxidation in sulfate-poor settings and refine global methane budgets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying an electrometallurgy analogy to treat the AOM consortium as an electrochemical system, using poised-potential electrochemistry to directly probe for non-sulfate electron sinks—a departure from traditional geochemical incubation studies that assume sulfate's monopoly.

proposals.title: Quantifying the role of iron and manganese oxides as potential electron acceptors for anaerobic oxidation of methane in microbial mats
proposals.motivation: Anaerobic oxidation of methane (AOM) is a critical biogeochemical process that regulates methane flux from marine sediments. It is predominantly coupled to sulfate reduction, but in environments where sulfate is depleted or limited, alternative electron acceptors could sustain AOM, influencing global methane budgets. Pre-2026 studies have suggested thermodynamic feasibility for metal oxide-driven AOM, yet direct rate measurements and in-situ evidence from complex natural systems like microbial mats are lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in the sulfate-depleted micro-niches of active AOM mats, particulate iron(III) and manganese(IV) oxides serve as alternative, quantifiable electron sinks for methane oxidation. This metal-dependent AOM operates at a significant fraction of the rate of sulfate-dependent AOM.
proposals.method: We will conduct slurry incubations with intact mat samples under anoxic conditions with 13C-labeled methane, systematically omitting sulfate and adding specific electron acceptors: ferrihydrite, amorphous MnO2, and nitrate. Sulfate reduction rates (SRR) will be measured via 35S-sulfate tracer. Potential metal reduction coupled to AOM will be quantified by monitoring 13C-DIC production and the depletion of Fe(III) and Mn(IV) via sequential chemical extraction and ICP-MS analysis. Cell-specific activity will be assessed using nanoSIMS on samples hybridized with ANME- and partner-specific FISH probes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct rate measurements for metal-dependent AOM in a complex natural mat system, challenging the paradigm of sulfate as the sole relevant electron acceptor. It will constrain the biogeochemical significance of this process and refine models of carbon cycling in methane-rich sediments.
proposals.novelty: While metal-AOM has been proposed, this study uniquely integrates precise rate measurements (SRR, metal reduction) with cell-specific activity assays (nanoSIMS) in a single, well-characterized natural habitat to definitively test its quantitative importance against the canonical sulfate-driven process.

proposals.title: In Situ Quantification of Anaerobic Methane Oxidation-Driven Carbonate Precipitation via Benthic Eddy Covariance of Ion Fluxes
proposals.motivation: Anaerobic oxidation of methane (AOM) coupled to sulfate reduction is a major sink for methane in marine sediments and a key driver of authigenic carbonate formation, which sequesters carbon over geological timescales. However, current rate measurements rely on discrete sediment cores or laboratory incubations, which disrupt natural hydrodynamics and fail to capture the integrated, real-time precipitation flux. A method to quantify the net mineral precipitation rate in situ is critical for accurately assessing the carbon sink strength and biogeochemical feedbacks in methane-rich environments.
proposals.hypothesis: The high-frequency covariance between vertical fluid velocity and the concentration of calcium or bicarbonate ions in the benthic boundary layer directly above AOM-active microbial mats provides a robust, non-invasive measure of the net carbonate precipitation flux.
proposals.method: We will adapt the aquatic eddy covariance technique by deploying a custom sensor array within 0.5-1 m of the seabed over active methane seeps. The array will include an acoustic Doppler velocimeter for 3D flow (16 Hz) and solid-state microsensors (e.g., Ca2+ ISE, pH) with response times <1 second. Ion concentrations will be calibrated in situ. The vertical flux (F) of a target ion (c) will be calculated as F = w'c', where w' and c' are deviations from the mean vertical velocity and concentration, respectively, over a 15-minute averaging interval. Fluxes will be correlated with simultaneous measurements of sulfate reduction rates using radiotracer (35S-SO42-) injections into adjacent cores.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first direct, non-destructive field measurement of AOM-driven carbonate precipitation rates, integrating over natural spatial heterogeneity and flow dynamics. It will establish a novel link between microbial metabolic rates (sulfate reduction) and a key mineral product, advancing our quantitative understanding of the deep biosphere's role in the carbon cycle.
proposals.novelty: The application of the eddy covariance principle to quantify mineral precipitation fluxes, rather than gas or solute exchange, is a radical conceptual and methodological leap. It directly addresses the challenge of measuring a net precipitation rate in a dynamic, reactive boundary layer without enclosure.

proposals.title: Resolving the dynamic lipidome of ANME-1/Desulfosarcina consortia: time-resolved 13C-SIP and multi-omics reveal lipid synthesis, exchange, and modification pathways
proposals.motivation: Anaerobic oxidation of methane (AOM) is a key microbial process regulating methane flux from marine sediments. The syntrophic partnership between anaerobic methanotrophic archaea (ANME) and sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) is central to this process, but the molecular mechanisms of their metabolic coupling, particularly the dynamics of lipid synthesis and exchange, remain poorly understood. Static lipid inventories suggest shared or modified lipids, but the active synthesis and transfer pathways are unknown, limiting our understanding of energy conservation and syntrophy in this globally important consortium.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ANME-1 and its partner SRB dynamically synthesize, exchange, and co-modify specific lipid classes during active AOM, and that these lipid dynamics are directly linked to the expression of specific biosynthetic and lipid-modifying enzyme systems.
proposals.method: We will cultivate ANME-1/Desulfosarcina consortia from Black Sea microbial mats in continuous-flow bioreactors under in-situ-like conditions, using 13C-labeled methane and bicarbonate. We will perform a high-resolution time-series sampling for lipid-SIP analysis via LC-MS/MS to track 13C incorporation into intact polar and core lipids. Concurrently, we will extract RNA and proteins for metatranscriptomic (Illumina sequencing) and metaproteomic (LC-MS/MS) analysis. Data integration will link the temporal patterns of lipid labeling with the expression profiles of key genes (e.g., for tetraether lipid synthesis in ANME, fatty acid biosynthesis in SRB, and putative lipid modification enzymes).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dynamic map of lipid metabolism in AOM consortia, directly identifying lipids unique to each partner and those subject to exchange or modification. It will establish a mechanistic link between lipid structures, their biosynthetic pathways, and the active metabolic state of the consortium, fundamentally advancing our understanding of carbon flow and syntrophic interaction in the deep subseafloor biosphere.
proposals.novelty: While lipid inventories of ANME/SRB consortia exist, this study is novel in applying time-resolved lipid-SIP within active bioreactors coupled with multi-omics to resolve the *kinetics* of lipid production and exchange, moving beyond a static snapshot to a process-oriented understanding.

proposals.title: Geochemical Control of Anaerobic Methane Oxidation: Quantifying Consortia Metabolic Rates and Mineral Precipitation Under Dynamic Environmental Gradients
proposals.motivation: Anaerobic oxidation of methane (AOM) mediated by syntrophic consortia of archaea and sulfate-reducing bacteria is a key biogeochemical process, regulating methane flux from marine sediments and forming authigenic carbonate reefs. However, quantitative links between porewater geochemistry (e.g., sulfate, methane, iron), the in situ metabolic rate of these slow-growing consortia, and the resulting mineral precipitation remain poorly constrained, limiting our ability to predict AOM's role in the deep biosphere and its geological record.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AOM consortia activity and carbonate precipitation are non-linearly controlled by the stoichiometry and absolute concentrations of electron donors (CH4) and acceptors (SO4²⁻), and that the presence of reactive iron phases can modulate this relationship by competing for sulfide and altering local pH.
proposals.method: We will conduct laboratory incubations with intact sediment cores and synthetic mats containing active AOM consortia from known seep sites. Using radiotracer (³⁵S-SO4²⁻, ¹⁴C-CH4) and stable isotope (¹³C-CH4) techniques, we will measure cell-specific sulfate reduction and methane oxidation rates under systematically varied gradients of sulfate (0.1-30 mM), methane (0.1-10 mM), and dissolved iron (Fe²⁺). Rates will be correlated with high-resolution microsensor profiles (pH, H2S) and post-incubation mineralogical analysis (SEM, XRD) to link metabolism to carbonate and iron-sulfide precipitation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework linking geochemical gradients to the metabolic rate and mineral-forming activity of AOM consortia. It will refine global estimates of sub-seafloor methane consumption and offer a mechanistic understanding of how ancient seep carbonate formations reflect past fluid chemistry and microbial activity.
proposals.novelty: While AOM rates have been measured, the explicit, simultaneous quantification of how dynamic multi-ion geochemistry controls both consortium metabolism and the resulting mineral assemblage in experimental mats is novel, moving beyond single-parameter studies.

proposals.title: Tracing the fate of methane-derived carbon in sulfate-methane transition zone sediments using 13C-CH4 stable isotope probing
proposals.motivation: In marine sediments, the anaerobic oxidation of methane (AOM) coupled to sulfate reduction is a major sink for methane, yet the quantitative flow of methane carbon into microbial biomass, specific biomolecules, and metabolic byproducts remains poorly constrained. Understanding this carbon flow is critical for accurately modeling carbon cycling in the sub-seafloor biosphere and assessing the metabolic strategies that support life in deep, energy-limited sediments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in sulfate-methane transition zone sediments, methane-derived carbon is preferentially incorporated into specific lipid biomarkers and microbial proteins of the AOM consortium, and that a significant fraction enters fermentation pathways, supporting a broader microbial network.
proposals.method: We will conduct laboratory incubations of intact sediment cores from known sulfate-methane transition zones with 13C-labeled methane (13C-CH4). Using nanoSIMS and compound-specific isotope analysis (CSIA) of lipid biomarkers (e.g., archaeol, sn2-hydroxyarchaeol), we will quantify 13C-enrichment in specific microbial groups. Proteomic stable isotope probing (Protein-SIP) will be applied to identify and quantify 13C incorporation into metabolic enzymes, distinguishing between pathways for direct methane assimilation and cross-feeding. Rates of sulfate reduction and methane oxidation will be measured in parallel using radiotracer (35SO42-) and pressure-based methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative mapping of methane carbon into the biomolecular inventory of the AOM consortium and associated fermentative community. It will constrain the efficiency of carbon assimilation versus oxidation to CO2 and reveal the metabolic connectivity between methane oxidizers and sulfate reducers in deep sediments.
proposals.novelty: While SIP studies have tracked methane oxidizers, the combined application of lipid-SIP and Protein-SIP to deep sediment AOM consortia to resolve pathway-specific carbon flow is novel. This moves beyond identifying active cells to quantifying the metabolic partitioning of methane carbon.

proposals.title: Predictive mapping of microbial functional niches in marine sediments by co-kriging of in situ microsensor gradients and 16S rRNA amplicon data
proposals.motivation: The metabolic rates and survival of subsurface microbes are governed by steep, microscale chemical gradients. However, traditional coring and sectioning destroys these gradients and provides only low-resolution snapshots, creating a disconnect between geochemical zonation and microbial spatial distribution. A method to statistically predict microbial community structure from continuous, in situ chemical measurements is needed to quantitatively link microenvironments to microbial biogeography.
proposals.hypothesis: That the spatial distribution of microbial functional groups (e.g., sulfate reducers, methanogens, sulfide oxidizers) in surface sediments can be accurately predicted by modeling them as co-regionalized variables with high-resolution in situ chemical parameters (O2, H2S, pH, CH4) using geostatistical interpolation.
proposals.method: We will deploy a motorized microprofiler with Clark-type O2, amperometric H2S, and potentiometric pH and CH4 microsensors to collect dense (100-200 μm vertical resolution) 2D chemical grids in coastal sediments. These data will be interpolated using ordinary kriging to create continuous chemical landscape maps. Subsequently, sediment cores will be sectioned at high resolution for 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing and geochemical analysis. Microbial community composition (e.g., relative abundance of key taxa) will be interpolated between physical sampling points using co-kriging, with the kriged chemical maps as covariates, employing a linear model of coregionalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first statistically rigorous, spatially explicit maps linking defined chemical niches to microbial taxa at sub-millimeter scales. It will move beyond correlation to provide a predictive framework for microbial distribution based on environmental parameters, directly testing the control of chemical gradients on community assembly. The method will set a new standard for quantifying microbial habitat space in sediments.
proposals.novelty: While microsensor profiling and microbial sequencing are established, their integration via geostatistical co-kriging to create predictive maps is novel. This approach treats microbial community data as a spatially continuous variable dependent on the chemical field, moving beyond simple point-wise correlation to spatial prediction.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Controls on Subseafloor Remineralization: A Test of the Primacy of Organic Matter Reactivity over Bulk Flux
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in deep biosphere research posits that the flux of organic matter (OM) to sediments is the primary control on microbial metabolic rates. However, this fails to fully explain the persistence of microbial activity in deeply buried, energy-starved sediments where bulk OM flux is negligible. A paradigm shift is needed to understand the long-term survival and biogeochemical function of the deep subseafloor biosphere, which is a key component of Earth's carbon cycle.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the intrinsic reactivity and bioavailability of the residual organic matter pool, governed by its molecular composition and association with mineral phases (e.g., iron oxides), is a stronger determinant of in-situ sulfate reduction and fermentation rates than the historical bulk OM flux.
proposals.method: We will conduct a comparative study of sediment cores from sites with contrasting OM fluxes (e.g., Peru Margin vs. North Pacific Gyre). We will quantify process rates using radiotracer (35S-sulfate, 14C-acetate) incubations at in-situ pressure/temperature. These will be correlated with high-resolution geochemical profiles of porewater (SO4, Fe2+, acetate) and solid-phase analyses, including sequential iron extraction and pyrolysis-GC/MS for OM characterization. A key analytical focus will be on quantifying the pool of mineral-associated, potentially bioavailable OM using oxidative degradation techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework linking specific OM properties to microbial energy yields in the deep biosphere. It will challenge the simple flux-based models of subsurface metabolism and offer a mechanistic explanation for the observed decoupling between sedimentation history and present-day metabolic activity, refining global estimates of subseafloor carbon turnover.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the classic assumption by treating OM quality and protection as the independent variable controlling rates, rather than a secondary modifier of bulk flux. It directly tests whether the 'reactivity continuum' concept, applied to surface sediments, governs metabolic survival over geological timescales in the deep subsurface.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Oxygen Sink: Coupling Diffusive and Total Oxygen Uptake to Anaerobic Carbon Oxidation Pathways in Coastal Sediments
proposals.motivation: Oxygen uptake at the sediment-water interface is a master variable for benthic carbon cycling, integrating aerobic respiration and the re-oxidation of reduced metabolites from anaerobic pathways like sulfate and iron reduction. Pre-2026, a key knowledge gap is the quantitative linkage between total oxygen demand (TOU) and the specific anaerobic processes driving the diffusive oxygen uptake (DOU). Resolving this is critical for accurately modeling carbon burial efficiency and understanding how changing organic matter inputs affect sediment biogeochemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in organic-rich coastal sediments, the discrepancy between TOU (measured by core incubations) and DOU (calculated from microprofiles) is primarily driven by the re-oxidation of reduced iron and sulfur species, and that this 'hidden' oxygen sink correlates with the depth-integrated rates of sulfate reduction and iron reduction.
proposals.method: We will collect intact sediment cores from a gradient of organic matter reactivity. High-resolution porewater oxygen microprofiles will be obtained ex situ using Clark-type microelectrodes to calculate DOU via Fick's first law. Parallel core incubations in temperature-controlled chambers with optode-based continuous oxygen monitoring will yield TOU. These measurements will be coupled with radiotracer (35SO42-) assays for sulfate reduction rates, porewater analyses for Fe2+, and sequential iron extractions to quantify reactive iron pools and reduction rates. A 1D reactive-transport model will be used to reconcile DOU, TOU, and anaerobic rate data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, rate-constrained framework linking the sediment oxygen sink to underlying anaerobic carbon oxidation pathways. It will yield new algorithms to predict TOU from DOU and porewater chemistry, improving global benthic flux estimates. The data will directly test the concept of 'metabolic flexibility' in the subsurface biosphere by showing how oxygen demand shifts with organic matter reactivity.
proposals.novelty: While DOU and TOU are commonly measured, their systematic deconvolution in relation to quantified rates of specific anaerobic processes (sulfate/iron reduction) in the same sediments is novel. The integrated use of microprofiling, core incubations, and radiotracer rate assays within a reactive-transport framework offers a non-obvious, holistic test of sediment redox coupling.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Controls: Linking Sedimentary Organic Matter Reactivity and Cell Abundance to Sulfate Reduction Rates in Deep Subseafloor Sediments
proposals.motivation: The deep subseafloor biosphere is a vast reservoir of microbial life, but the factors controlling its metabolic rates and survival remain poorly constrained. A key knowledge gap is the quantitative relationship between the availability and reactivity of buried organic matter, the standing stock of microbial cells, and the process rates of terminal electron acceptors like sulfate. Resolving these relationships is critical for predicting the magnitude and distribution of subsurface carbon cycling and microbial persistence over geological timescales.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sulfate reduction rates in deep marine sediments are quantitatively predictable from a combination of the concentration and intrinsic reactivity of sedimentary organic matter and the in situ abundance of microbial cells, with organic reactivity being the primary master variable.
proposals.method: We will analyze a global dataset of deep sediment cores (>10mbsf) from ODP/IODP expeditions, integrating pre-existing and new measurements. Organic matter availability and reactivity will be characterized by total organic carbon (TOC), C/N ratios, and Rock-Eval pyrolysis parameters (Hydrogen Index, Oxygen Index). These will be correlated with depth-resolved, cell-specific sulfate reduction rates (SRR) measured via 35S-radiotracer incubations and total cell counts from fluorescence microscopy (SYBR Green II staining). Statistical modeling (multiple linear regression and random forest algorithms) will be used to identify the dominant predictors of SRR.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, mechanistic framework for predicting sulfate reduction rates from measurable sediment properties in the deep biosphere. It will directly test the 'metabolic rate of the biosphere' concept by linking bulk geochemistry to cellular-scale activity, refining global estimates of subsurface carbon oxidation.
proposals.novelty: While organic matter and cell counts are often reported, their combined predictive power for process rates in the deep subsurface is untested. This study moves beyond correlation to develop a predictive model by explicitly incorporating organic matter *reactivity* parameters, a key advance over simple concentration-based approaches.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Metabolic Landscape: Are Non-Standard Distributions of Sulfate Reduction and Fermentation the Norm in Deep Subseafloor Sediments?
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies, including those by Jørgensen and colleagues, have established a 'standard model' for microbial metabolism in deep marine sediments, where sulfate reduction dominates organic carbon oxidation in the sulfate zone, followed by methanogenesis. However, recent rate measurements from specific sites (e.g., hot, deep biosphere) reveal significant deviations, with fermentation sometimes rivaling or exceeding sulfate reduction. It is critical to determine if these deviations are rare anomalies or indicative of a more complex, heterogeneous global subsurface ecosystem, challenging our fundamental understanding of carbon and sulfur cycling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the reported deviations from the standard model of metabolic distribution are not exceptions but represent a common, yet under-sampled, state in deep subseafloor sediments, driven by site-specific variations in organic matter reactivity, iron mineralogy, and thermodynamic constraints.
proposals.method: We will conduct a meta-analysis of published cell-specific sulfate reduction (SRR) and fermentation rates from global subseafloor sediment cores (IODP/ODP), focusing on depths >10 mbsf. New, targeted rate measurements will be performed on archived sediment samples from contrasting biogeochemical provinces (e.g., organic-rich margins vs. oligotrophic gyres) using radiotracer (35S-SO4) assays for SRR and stable isotope probing (13C-labeled substrates) combined with NanoSIMS to quantify fermentation rates at the single-cell level. Statistical analysis (e.g., principal component analysis and regression modeling) will relate metabolic ratios to environmental parameters (organic carbon quality, porewater sulfate/iron, temperature).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, global assessment of the prevalence of non-standard metabolic regimes in the deep biosphere. It will directly test the universality of current biogeochemical models and deliver a revised framework that incorporates fermentation as a potentially major carbon flow, thereby improving predictions of subsurface carbon remineralization and methane production.
proposals.novelty: While deviations have been noted, no systematic study has attempted to quantify their global frequency or underlying drivers. This proposal moves from documenting single-site anomalies to statistically evaluating their commonality, integrating rate measurements with geochemical parameters to explain why the standard model fails in certain environments.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Flux and Reactivity of Sediment-Derived Electron Donors to the Subseafloor Basaltic Aquifer
proposals.motivation: The deep subseafloor basaltic aquifer is a vast, hydrologically active microbial habitat, but the sources and fluxes of electron donors that fuel its biosphere are poorly constrained. Sediments overlying basaltic crust are a potential major source of labile organic carbon and reduced chemical species (e.g., Fe(II), NH4+, CH4). Quantifying this sediment-to-crust flux is critical for understanding the total metabolic rate and biogeochemical function of the deep, hot subseafloor biosphere, a key component of Earth's carbon and sulfur cycles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the diffusive and advective flux of dissolved electron donors from organic-rich sediments constitutes a primary energy source for microbial communities in the underlying basaltic crust, and that the composition of this flux controls the dominant terminal electron-accepting processes in the crustal aquifer.
proposals.method: We will deploy novel in-situ porewater samplers (e.g., 'U-tube' samplers or diffusive equilibration samplers) at Integrated Ocean Drilling Program (IODP) borehole observatories (CORKs) to collect time-series samples from the sediment-basement interface. Concentrations of key electron donors (dissolved organic carbon (DOC), volatile fatty acids, H2, CH4, Fe(II), NH4+) will be measured via standard shipboard and shore-based analytical techniques (e.g., HPLC, GC, spectrophotometry). The diffusive flux will be calculated from concentration gradients across the sediment-basement boundary using Fick's first law, while advective flux will be estimated from measured or modeled fluid flow rates. Parallel measurements of sulfate reduction rates (via 35S-radiotracer) and carbon oxidation rates in the deepest sediments will constrain the sediment's internal reactivity and net export potential.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative assessment of the electron donor subsidy from sediments to the crustal biosphere, directly linking sedimentary and crustal biogeochemical cycles. It will establish a fundamental mass balance for energy flow into the deep basaltic aquifer, refining global models of subseafloor microbial productivity and its dependence on overlying sediment type and diagenetic state.
proposals.novelty: While sediment biogeochemistry and crustal hydrology are well-studied in isolation, this proposal directly targets the critical interface between them, applying Jørgensen's established rate-measurement frameworks to a novel, spatially constrained flux problem at a major biome boundary.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Flux and Microbial Utilization of Photosynthetically Fixed Carbon to Basal Sedimentary Communities
proposals.motivation: Deep subseafloor sediments host a vast, slow-growing microbial biosphere whose energy and carbon sources remain enigmatic. While geochemical gradients suggest organic matter degradation over millions of years, the extent to which fresh, surface-derived photosynthetic carbon fuels these deepest communities is unknown. Quantifying this flux is critical for understanding the global carbon cycle and the energetic limits of life in the deep biosphere.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a small but continuous flux of reactive, photosynthetically fixed carbon, transported via pore fluids or as part of a labile organic matter fraction, sustains basal microbial communities and their associated sulfate and iron reduction processes.
proposals.method: We will conduct shipboard incubations of intact sediment cores from a deep, organic-poor site (e.g., South Pacific Gyre) using 13C-bicarbonate tracer injected at the sediment-water interface. The downward propagation of the label will be tracked over time through porewater dissolved inorganic carbon (DIC), specific low-molecular-weight organic acids, and bulk microbial biomass via NanoSIMS analysis of sorted cells. Concurrent measurements of sulfate reduction rates (via 35S-SO42− radiotracer) and iron reduction will link carbon assimilation to terminal electron-accepting processes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct quantification of the vertical transfer efficiency of modern photosynthetic carbon to the deep sedimentary biosphere. It will constrain the reactivity and turnover of this carbon pool and reveal the metabolic coupling between surface production and deep microbial survival, refining global models of subseafloor carbon cycling.
