proposals.novelty: While isotopic labeling is common in surface sediments, applying it to trace carbon through the entire sediment column to million-year-old communities is novel. The combined approach of tracking the label into both specific metabolic products and the biomass of deep, slow-growing cells via NanoSIMS is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Quantifying the impact of basaltic aquifer fluid exchange on subseafloor carbon oxidation pathways and microbial metabolic rates
proposals.motivation: Recent discoveries of fluid exchange between the basaltic oceanic crust and overlying sediments challenge the paradigm of diffusive-dominated solute transport in the deep biosphere. This advective supply of electron acceptors (e.g., sulfate) and donors from a vast aquifer could fundamentally alter the reactivity of buried organic matter and the metabolic rates of subsurface microbial communities, with major implications for global biogeochemical cycles. Pre-2026, there is no quantitative, process-based model that couples aquifer hydrology with detailed sediment biogeochemistry to assess this impact.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fluid exchange from the basaltic aquifer sustains elevated microbial metabolic rates in overlying sediments by supplying limiting electron acceptors, thereby shifting the dominant carbon oxidation pathways from iron reduction to sulfate reduction and increasing the overall oxidation of recalcitrant organic carbon.
proposals.method: We will develop a 1D reactive-transport model coupling a sediment column to a basaltic aquifer layer. The model will integrate a hydrological module for fluid exchange (Darcy flow) with a biogeochemical module simulating the major terminal electron-accepting processes (TEAPs: sulfate, iron, and manganese reduction). It will use pre-2026 kinetic frameworks (e.g., Monod-type rate laws) for organic carbon degradation and microbial respiration, parameterized with site-specific rate measurements from North Pond and other ridge-flank systems. The model will be implemented in COMSOL Multiphysics or a custom finite-difference code to solve the coupled advection-diffusion-reaction equations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework to predict how aquifer-sediment exchange influences deep biosphere metabolism and carbon cycling. It will yield testable predictions for cell-specific sulfate reduction rates and carbon oxidation capacities under varying fluid fluxes, directly informing future drilling and sampling campaigns. The model will establish a new mechanistic link between crustal hydrology and sediment biogeochemistry.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, process-based coupling of aquifer hydrology with a multi-electron acceptor biogeochemical model for sediments, moving beyond simple diffusion models or geochemical tracer studies to mechanistically predict microbial metabolic rates driven by crustal fluid flow.

proposals.title: Microfluidic Gradients Reveal the Coupling of Sulfate Reduction, Iron Cycling, and Organic Carbon Reactivity in Coastal Sediments
proposals.motivation: A major challenge in geomicrobiology is quantifying microbial metabolic rates under realistic, spatially structured chemical conditions. In situ gradients of sulfate, dissolved organic carbon, and Fe(II) in sediments are critical for controlling the pathways and rates of anaerobic carbon mineralization, but traditional slurry incubations homogenize these gradients, potentially distorting microbial activity and interactions. This limits our ability to predict carbon preservation and sulfur/iron cycling in dynamic coastal environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the rate and pathway of microbial organic carbon oxidation in sediments are non-linearly controlled by the steepness and overlap of opposing sulfate and dissolved organic carbon gradients, with Fe(II) fluxes modulating sulfate reduction efficiency. This coupling is best captured in a diffusion-controlled system that mimics the sediment matrix.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate microfluidic 'gradient chips' using soft lithography with PDMS, creating adjacent, diffusion-coupled channels. One channel will be perfused with sulfate-rich seawater, the other with defined organic substrates (e.g., acetate, monomers). Anoxic coastal sediment slurries, characterized for their iron and organic carbon content, will be injected into a central chamber. Using time-lapse microscopy and geochemical sampling ports, we will map the establishment of chemical gradients via microsensors (e.g., for sulfide, Fe(II)) and quantify process rates. Cell-specific activity will be assessed post-experiment via FISH-microautoradiography or nanoSIMS using 35S-sulfate and 13C-labeled organics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, spatially resolved measurements of sulfate reduction and fermentation rates under controlled, environmentally relevant gradient conditions. It will quantify how diffusive substrate supply, rather than bulk concentration, controls metabolic rates and microbial niches. The results will refine conceptual and quantitative models of carbon cycling in subsurface sediments, linking microbial ecology to geochemistry.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics is used in pure culture studies, its application to complex natural sediment communities to deconvolve gradient-controlled processes is novel. This approach moves beyond black-box rate measurements to visualize and quantify how the physical architecture of the sediment pore network governs biogeochemical reactions.

proposals.title: Linking Phylogeny to Metabolic Function in Deep Subseafloor Sediments via SIP-SIMS: Quantifying Single-Cell Sulfate Reduction and Iron Oxidation
proposals.motivation: Deep subseafloor sediments host a vast, slow-growing microbial biosphere that drives key biogeochemical cycles, including sulfate reduction and iron redox transformations. However, pre-2026 methods like bulk rate measurements and 16S rRNA gene surveys cannot directly link the phylogenetic identity of individual cells to their specific metabolic activities, creating a critical gap in understanding the functional drivers of deep subsurface ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant fraction of deep sediment microbial communities are metabolically active, and that SIP-SIMS will reveal phylogenetically distinct sub-populations with divergent metabolic strategies, specifically identifying cells actively reducing sulfate versus those oxidizing organic carbon via iron reduction or fermentation.
proposals.method: We will incubate intact sediment cores from sulfate-methane transition zones (IODP samples) with stable isotope-labeled substrates (13C-acetate, 18O-sulfate, 57Fe-oxides). Following incubation, cells will be extracted, sorted via fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS), and subjected to NanoSIMS analysis to quantify 13C, 34S, and 57Fe incorporation at the single-cell level. Subsequently, phylogenetic identity of the same sorted, active cells will be determined via single-cell genomics or targeted 16S rRNA gene sequencing after whole-genome amplification, creating a direct activity-identity link.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative measurements of substrate uptake and transformation rates by individual microbial cells within the deep sedimentary biosphere. It will resolve which phylogenetic groups are the primary agents of sulfate reduction and iron cycling in situ, fundamentally advancing our understanding of energy flow and carbon remineralization in the deep subsurface.
proposals.novelty: While SIP-SIMS has been applied in surface environments, its combination with single-cell sorting and genomics to deconvolve the functional roles of specific lineages in deep, energy-limited sediments is novel and addresses a long-standing limitation in subsurface biogeochemistry.

proposals.title: Inverting the Deep Biosphere: Reconstructing Past Export Productivity from the Metabolic Signatures of Deep-Sea Sediment Communities
proposals.motivation: Quantifying past ocean primary productivity is crucial for understanding the carbon cycle's role in climate change, but existing paleo-proxies (e.g., alkenones, biogenic opal) have significant uncertainties and preservation biases. The deep subseafloor biosphere, fueled by the burial of organic matter from the surface ocean, contains a vast and slow-turnover microbial community whose composition and metabolic rates are intrinsically linked to the quantity and quality of this organic input over millennia. This project proposes to use this deep biosphere as a novel, integrated recorder of past export production.
proposals.hypothesis: The contemporary community composition and depth-integrated metabolic rates of sulfate-reducing and fermentative microorganisms in deep marine sediments can be quantitatively linked to the magnitude and reactivity of organic carbon flux to the seafloor in the past, allowing the reconstruction of paleo-productivity.
proposals.method: We will integrate existing and new data from sediment cores (e.g., from the Baltic Sea, Peru Margin). The method involves: 1) Measuring cell-specific sulfate reduction and fermentation rates using radiotracer (35-SO4, 14-C-acetate) techniques at high depth resolution. 2) Quantifying microbial community composition via 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing, focusing on sulfate-reducer (e.g., Desulfobacterota) and fermenter lineages. 3) Developing an inverse model based on a reaction-transport framework. The model will treat the contemporary microbial data as the observed state and iteratively solve for the historical organic carbon deposition flux that best reproduces the observed depth profiles of community structure and metabolic activity, constrained by sediment age models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, biologically grounded paleo-proxy that integrates organic carbon delivery over time, complementing and testing geochemical proxies. It will provide a quantitative link between the deep biosphere's present state and past ocean surface conditions, advancing our understanding of long-term carbon sequestration in marine sediments.
proposals.novelty: While microbial processes are known to shape sedimentary records, using the *current* state of the deep biosphere as a quantitative archive to inversely model *past* surface inputs is a fundamentally new approach. It moves beyond correlative studies to treat the subseafloor community as a dynamic paleo-sensor.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Geological Energy Subsidy: The Flux and Microbial Utilization of Abiotically Derived H2, CH4, and H2S in Deep-Sea Sediments
proposals.motivation: The deep subseafloor biosphere is energy-limited, with microbial communities primarily fueled by the burial and degradation of organic matter. However, geological processes like serpentinization and thermogenic reactions can supply reduced compounds (H2, CH4, H2S) independently of surface primary production. The magnitude and significance of this geological energy flux to the total seafloor energy budget remains poorly quantified, limiting our understanding of global subsurface carbon cycling and life in extreme environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that geological transport of reduced compounds supplies a significant and previously underestimated fraction of the total energy flux available to microbial communities in hydrothermally influenced and tectonically active deep-sea sediments, sustaining sulfate reduction and methanogenesis beyond levels supported by buried organic matter alone.
proposals.method: We will integrate geochemical flux measurements with microbial rate assays. Porewater profiles (sulfate, sulfide, methane, hydrogen) and sediment characteristics from drill cores will be used to model diffusive and advective fluxes. These will be combined with direct, radiotracer-based measurements of sulfate reduction rates (35S-SO4) and methanogenesis rates (14C-labeled substrates) on the same samples. A key analytical step will be the use of specific metabolic inhibitors (e.g., molybdate for sulfate reduction) in incubation experiments to partition the oxidation of geological versus organically derived substrates. The energy flux (in Joules per area per time) from each geological compound will be calculated from its oxidation potential and measured consumption rate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated, rate-based quantification of the geological energy subsidy to seafloor sediments. It will directly test the 'metabolic rate' of the biosphere in settings where geology provides an alternative energy source, refining global models of subsurface carbon and sulfur cycling. The data will constrain the habitability and survival strategies of the deep biosphere in energy-poor old sediments near geological interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While the existence of geological substrates is known, their quantitative contribution to the *in situ* microbial energy budget is unresolved. This proposal moves beyond geochemical inventories to directly link geological flux measurements with microbial metabolic rate assays, partitioning energy sources in a manner not previously achieved.

proposals.title: Specificity and Side Effects of Molybdate Inhibition in Marine Sediments: Resolving Impacts on Sulfate Reduction, Methanogenesis, and Microbial Community Structure
proposals.motivation: Molybdate is a widely used specific inhibitor of sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) in biogeochemical rate measurements, crucial for quantifying sulfate reduction and its coupling to organic carbon oxidation and iron cycling in sediments. However, its assumed specificity and potential side effects on methanogens and other anaerobic microbes are not fully resolved, risking misinterpretation of process rates and microbial interactions in critical anoxic environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that molybdate, at concentrations typically used to inhibit SRB, will not significantly inhibit methanogenic activity but may induce shifts in the broader fermentative and syntrophic community, thereby indirectly altering carbon flow and electron acceptor utilization.
proposals.method: We will conduct slurry incubations with coastal and deep-sea sediments, applying a gradient of molybdate concentrations alongside controls. Process rates will be quantified via radiotracer (35S-sulfate) assays for sulfate reduction and gas chromatography for methane production. Community-wide metabolic responses will be assessed by measuring dissolved organic carbon fermentation products (e.g., acetate, H2). Microbial community structure shifts will be characterized via 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing, with a focus on SRB (e.g., Desulfobacterota), methanogens (e.g., Methanomicrobia), and fermentative taxa.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, quantitative framework for the appropriate use of molybdate in sediment biogeochemistry, clarifying its specificity. It will advance our understanding of microbial metabolic networks by revealing how inhibition of a key terminal electron-accepting process reshapes carbon flow and community interactions in subseafloor sediments.
proposals.novelty: While molybdate inhibition is standard, a systematic investigation coupling high-resolution rate measurements of both sulfate reduction and methanogenesis with community profiling under inhibition is lacking. This directly tests a foundational methodological assumption in the study of subsurface biosphere metabolism.

proposals.title: Seasonal Dynamics of Concurrent Methanogenesis and Sulfate Reduction in Coastal Surface Sediments: Resolving Temporal Variability in Metabolic Rates and Carbon Flow
proposals.motivation: In marine sediments, sulfate reduction and methanogenesis are key terminal organic carbon mineralization processes, typically separated in space due to thermodynamic competition. However, their potential co-occurrence in surface sediments, driven by fresh organic matter input or fluctuating redox conditions, remains poorly quantified over time. Understanding the temporal dynamics of these competing pathways is crucial for accurately modeling carbon cycling, methane fluxes, and microbial survival strategies in dynamic coastal environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the rates of methanogenesis and sulfate reduction in surface sediments exhibit strong seasonal or episodic variability, with methanogenesis becoming periodically significant during phases of high organic matter reactivity and transient sulfate depletion, challenging the classical vertical zonation model.
proposals.method: We will conduct a high-resolution, year-long time-series sampling at a well-characterized coastal site (e.g., Aarhus Bay). Concurrent rates of sulfate reduction and methanogenesis will be quantified in the top 0-10 cm using radiotracer techniques (35S-SO42- and 14C-bicarbonate/14C-acetate) combined with sediment slurry incubations. Porewater chemistry (SO42-, CH4, Fe2+, DOC) and solid-phase parameters (organic carbon content, reactivity proxies) will be analyzed. Rate measurements will be integrated with a reactive-transport model to resolve the temporal coupling between carbon oxidation, iron cycling, and electron acceptor availability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, temporally resolved dataset on the co-dynamics of these key metabolic pathways in surface sediments. It will refine global carbon cycle models by constraining the timing and magnitude of methane production in sulfate-rich zones and elucidate how microbial communities and their metabolic rates respond to seasonal forcing.
proposals.novelty: While individual process rates are well-studied, the systematic, concurrent measurement of their temporal variability in surface sediments to capture episodic decoupling of the sulfate-methane transition zone is novel. This moves beyond steady-state assumptions to a dynamic view of microbial competition and carbon flow.

proposals.title: Quantifying Carbon Partitioning in Marine Sediments: A Reaction-Transport Model for the Co-Occurrence of Sulfate Reduction and Methanogenesis
proposals.motivation: In marine sediments, thermodynamic theory predicts that sulfate reduction should outcompete methanogenesis for substrates like hydrogen and acetate, yet geochemical and microbiological data consistently show their co-occurrence in sulfate-rich zones. This paradox challenges our understanding of carbon flow and microbial interactions in the sub-seafloor biosphere. Resolving it is critical for accurately modeling global carbon cycling and methane production in ocean margins.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the co-occurrence is sustained by the physical isolation of microbial consortia and the differential reactivity of organic matter pools, creating micro-niches that allow methanogens to access non-competitive substrates (e.g., methylated compounds) even during active sulfate reduction.
proposals.method: We will develop a one-dimensional, multi-G reaction-transport model using pre-2026 frameworks (e.g., as in Jørgensen & Parkes, 2010). The model will incorporate discrete pools of organic carbon with varying reactivities (G1, G2) and explicitly track the flow of carbon to terminal electron acceptors (sulfate, CO2). Key algorithms will include Michaelis-Menten kinetics for microbial processes and a diffusion term for solute transport. The model will be parameterized and validated against existing rate measurements (sulfate reduction, methanogenesis) and porewater chemistry from well-studied sites like Aarhus Bay.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, quantitative framework to predict carbon partitioning between these key pathways, moving beyond descriptive observations. It will directly inform global biogeochemical models by offering a validated parameterization for co-occurring anaerobic processes in subsurface sediments.
proposals.novelty: While models often treat sulfate reduction and methanogenesis as mutually exclusive, this proposal integrates substrate diversity and microbial micro-niches into a kinetic model to explicitly explain their co-existence, a synthesis not yet fully achieved in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Quantifying Carbon Partitioning and Metabolic Flux in Subseafloor Sediments: A 13C-MFA Approach to Resolve Competition Between Sulfate Reduction and Methanogenesis
proposals.motivation: In marine sediments, the terminal degradation of organic matter is partitioned between sulfate reduction and methanogenesis, a competition that globally regulates methane fluxes and carbon burial. Pre-2026 rate measurements quantify process magnitudes but cannot resolve the underlying metabolic network or carbon routing at the cellular level. Applying systems biology tools to this environmental problem is crucial to understand the biochemical basis of microbial survival and carbon flow in the deep biosphere.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that 13C metabolic flux analysis (13C-MFA) will reveal distinct, quantifiable patterns of carbon routing through central catabolic and anabolic pathways in sulfate-reducing and methanogenic communities, and that the relative flux towards each terminal process is controlled by the thermodynamic landscape and substrate quality.
proposals.method: We will incubate anoxic sediment slurries from sulfate-methane transition zones with position-specific 13C-labeled substrates (e.g., [1-13C]acetate, [2-13C]acetate, 13C-bicarbonate). Using established protocols for extracting intracellular metabolites from low-biomass sediments, we will measure 13C-labeling patterns in proteinogenic amino acids and central metabolites via GC-MS. Flux distributions will be calculated using computational 13C-MFA software (e.g., INCA, OpenFLUX) by fitting the labeling data to a comprehensive metabolic network model incorporating competing pathways for sulfate reduction, methanogenesis, and fermentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative flux map of carbon flow in subseafloor sedimentary communities, moving beyond net rates to internal metabolic dynamics. It will directly link geochemical gradients (sulfate, iron, organic reactivity) to intracellular biochemistry, offering a mechanistic framework to predict microbial responses to environmental change.
proposals.novelty: While 13C-MFA is standard in biotechnology, its application to complex, low-energy, natural sedimentary communities to resolve long-standing biogeochemical competition is novel. This bridges the conceptual gap between systems biology and geobiology by quantifying in situ metabolic strategies.

proposals.title: Mapping the Sulfate-Methane Transition Zone with In Situ Resistivity: A Non-Invasive Proxy for Microbial Metabolic Activity in Marine Sediments
proposals.motivation: The sulfate-methane transition zone (SMTZ) is a critical biogeochemical interface in marine sediments, where anaerobic oxidation of methane coupled to sulfate reduction (AOM) dominates carbon and sulfur cycling. Pre-2026, mapping its depth and variability requires coring and porewater analysis, which is labor-intensive and spatially discrete. A non-invasive geophysical method to map the SMTZ would revolutionize our ability to assess the spatial distribution and controls on deep subsurface microbial activity at the scale of entire sedimentary basins.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strong electrical conductivity contrast between sulfate-rich (high conductivity) and methane-rich (low conductivity) porewaters, amplified by the precipitation of authigenic minerals (e.g., iron sulfides) at the SMTZ, creates a detectable resistivity anomaly that can be used to map the SMTZ depth accurately across diverse sedimentary environments.
proposals.method: We will deploy a multi-electrode resistivity probe (e.g., a modified 2D Wenner array) during research cruises to obtain high-resolution, in situ resistivity depth profiles at stations with known SMTZ depths from traditional geochemical profiles. We will develop an inversion algorithm (e.g., a 1D Occam's inversion) to convert apparent resistivity to true formation resistivity. This resistivity model will be calibrated against direct measurements of porewater sulfate, methane, dissolved iron, and solid-phase iron sulfide content to establish a site-specific petrophysical relationship. The calibrated model will then be applied to new stations to predict SMTZ depth from resistivity alone.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish sediment resistivity as a quantitative, non-invasive proxy for the SMTZ, enabling rapid, high-spatial-resolution surveys of this key biogeochemical horizon. It will directly link geophysical properties to microbial metabolic rates (sulfate reduction and AOM) and carbon oxidation pathways, providing a novel tool to upscale point measurements of microbial activity to the sedimentary system scale.
proposals.novelty: While resistivity is used for gas hydrate and fluid flow studies, its systematic application as a direct proxy for the SMTZ depth, specifically calibrated against the coupled sulfate, methane, and iron-sulfur cycles central to Jørgensen's research, is novel. This bridges high-resolution geochemical rate measurements with basin-scale geophysical surveying.

proposals.title: Microscale Coexistence of Sulfate Reduction and Methanogenesis in Marine Aggregates: Resolving the Sulfate-Methane Transition Zone at its Fundamental Scale
proposals.motivation: In marine sediments, sulfate reduction and methanogenesis are typically separated into distinct zones due to thermodynamic competition, with the sulfate-methane transition zone (SMTZ) being a key biogeochemical interface. However, aggregate-scale microenvironments, like marine snow, may host concurrent processes, challenging the bulk sediment paradigm. Understanding this microscale concurrence is crucial for accurately modeling carbon mineralization pathways, methane fluxes, and microbial survival strategies in dynamic particulate systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that steep microscale chemical gradients within organic-rich aggregates create distinct microniches, allowing for the concurrent activity of sulfate-reducing bacteria and methanogenic archaea. Specifically, we posit that rapid sulfate depletion in aggregate cores, coupled with diffusive supply limitations, enables methanogenesis to initiate before bulk sulfate is exhausted.
proposals.method: We will incubate synthetic, isotopically-labeled (13C-acetate, DIC) marine snow aggregates under in-situ conditions. Using high-resolution microsensors (H2S, pH, O2) and laser ablation isotope ratio mass spectrometry, we will map chemical gradients and process rates at a 50-100 µm scale. Cell-specific sulfate reduction rates will be quantified via 35S-sulfate tracer incubations combined with catalyzed reporter deposition-fluorescence in situ hybridization (CARD-FISH), while methanogenesis rates will be determined via 14C-bicarbonate and 14C-acetate tracers and phase-separated liquid scintillation counting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, rate-constrained evidence for the coexistence of sulfate reduction and methanogenesis within individual marine aggregates. It will quantify the carbon flow partitioning between these pathways at the microscale, offering a mechanistic framework to upscale aggregate-driven carbon cycling. The findings will refine models of early diagenesis and the biological carbon pump in the water column and surface sediments.
proposals.novelty: While bulk sediment studies firmly establish the spatial separation of sulfate reduction and methanogenesis, this proposal explicitly tests their co-occurrence within the same, millimeter-scale biotic structure. The novel integration of process rate measurements with chemical imaging at the aggregate scale moves beyond correlative bulk phase descriptions to a causal, mechanistic understanding.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Global Role of Oxygen Minimum Zone Sediments in Marine Methane and Sulfide Fluxes
proposals.motivation: Oxygen minimum zones (OMZs) are critical marine biogeochemical provinces where intense organic matter remineralization drives anaerobic processes like sulfate and iron reduction. Pre-2026 studies have quantified process rates at specific sites, but a robust, spatially resolved global estimate of their contribution to benthic methane and sulfide fluxes is lacking. This gap hinders our ability to accurately model oceanic carbon-sulfur cycling and predict feedbacks on climate and ecosystem health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that OMZ sediments, due to enhanced organic carbon reactivity and suppressed aerobic oxidation, contribute disproportionately to global marine benthic methane and sulfide fluxes compared to their aerial extent. This contribution is modulated by the regional interplay of sulfate reduction, iron cycling, and methanogenesis.
proposals.method: We will compile a global database of published and new rate measurements (sulfate reduction, methanogenesis, iron reduction) from OMZ sediments, linked to key environmental parameters (bottom-water O2, organic carbon flux, sediment type). Using a machine learning regression algorithm (e.g., Random Forest), we will model the spatial distribution of these rates based on globally gridded environmental data. These modeled rates will be integrated over the global OMZ area, defined by dissolved oxygen thresholds (<20 µmol/kg), to calculate total fluxes. Uncertainty will be propagated via Monte Carlo simulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first data-driven, spatially explicit budget for methane and sulfide production from OMZ sediments, directly quantifying their role in the global marine carbon and sulfur cycles. It will identify key geographical hotspots and environmental controls, offering critical constraints for Earth system models and informing assessments of benthic-pelagic coupling and potential sulfide/methane release to the water column.
proposals.novelty: While site-specific studies exist, the novel scaling approach using a predictive, process-attributed model based on global datasets moves beyond simple area-weighting. It explicitly integrates the known reactivity of organic carbon and competitive microbial pathways (sulfate vs. iron reduction) to constrain methane yields in these sulfidic environments at a global scale.

proposals.title: Quantifying Microbial Sulfur Cycling in Ancient Euxinic Basins: A Mechanistic Isotope Model Constrained by Modern Sediment Process Rates
proposals.motivation: The biogeochemical interpretation of sulfur and carbon isotopes in ancient euxinic shales, key archives for ocean redox evolution, remains ambiguous. Pre-2026 studies often apply simplified fractionation factors, neglecting the dynamic interplay between sulfate reduction rates, organic matter reactivity, and iron-sulfur-organic carbon interactions observed in modern oxygen minimum zone (OMZ) sediments. A process-based model grounded in modern sedimentary biogeochemistry is needed to deconvolve the isotopic signals of microbial metabolism from those of later diagenesis and water column processes.
proposals.hypothesis: The isotopic composition (δ³⁴S, δ¹³C) of sulfur and carbon in ancient euxinic shales can be quantitatively explained by a model incorporating depth-dependent sulfate reduction rates, organic matter lability spectra, and competitive iron reduction, as constrained by observations from modern OMZ sediments.
proposals.method: We will develop a reactive-transport model for early diagenesis, parameterized with rate laws and fractionation factors from Jørgensen's pre-2026 work on cell-specific sulfate reduction and fermentation in deep sediments. The model will simulate sulfate reduction driven by a multi-G reactivity continuum of organic carbon, competitive Fe(III) reduction, and sulfide re-oxidation/sequestration. We will inversely fit the model to high-resolution δ³⁴S of pyrite and δ¹³C of organic carbon from well-studied Devonian-Mississippian shale sections using a Markov Chain Monte Carlo algorithm to constrain ancient rate distributions and organic carbon lability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework linking specific microbial metabolic processes to bulk sedimentary isotope records, moving beyond empirical correlations. It will deliver novel constraints on the pace and efficiency of the subsurface biosphere in ancient euxinic settings and refine the use of δ³⁴S and δ¹³C as proxies for paleo-productivity and redox conditions.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in explicitly translating quantitative, process-rate understanding from modern sub-seafloor biosphere research into a predictive model for ancient rock isotopes, directly testing the hypothesis that ancient isotopic patterns reflect known microbial metabolic dynamics rather than fundamentally different biogeochemistry.

proposals.title: Testing the Obligate Autotrophy of Marine Anammox Bacteria: A Combined Rate and Carbon Flow Study in Sulfate-Rich Sediments
proposals.motivation: The anaerobic oxidation of ammonium (anammox) is a key nitrogen loss process in marine sediments, often co-occurring with sulfate and iron reduction in organic-rich layers. Pre-2026 understanding posits marine anammox bacteria as strictly autotrophic, but this has not been rigorously tested under in situ-like conditions where organic carbon compounds from fermentation or other microbial processes are abundant. Resolving their metabolic flexibility is crucial for accurately modeling carbon and nitrogen cycling in sediments, as it determines whether anammox acts as a carbon sink or is coupled to organic matter degradation.
proposals.hypothesis: Marine anammox bacteria from continental margin sediments are obligate autotrophs and cannot assimilate or oxidize simple organic carbon compounds (e.g., acetate, formate, pyruvate) for growth or energy generation, even when ammonium is limiting.
proposals.method: We will incubate anammox-active sediment slurries from Aarhus Bay with 15N-labeled ammonium and sulfate, under anoxic conditions. Treatments will be amended with 13C-labeled organic substrates (acetate, formate) or 14C-bicarbonate. Anammox rates will be quantified via 15N2 production by membrane inlet mass spectrometry. Simultaneously, we will track the incorporation of 13C into bacterial lipids (via GC-MS) and bulk biomass to distinguish autotrophic versus heterotrophic carbon fixation. Cell-specific sulfate reduction rates (using 35S-sulfate) and fermentation products will be monitored to contextualize carbon availability and potential cross-feeding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide definitive, rate-quantified evidence on the carbon metabolism of environmentally relevant anammox bacteria. It will clarify their role in sediment carbon cycling—either as a decoupled, purely lithotrophic process or as a potentially mixotrophic one—refining biogeochemical models of coastal and marine systems. The study will also bridge Jørgensen's expertise in sulfate reduction and carbon oxidation with the ecology of the nitrogen cycle.
proposals.novelty: While anammox biochemistry suggests autotrophy, no pre-2026 study has directly tested the metabolic response of marine sediment anammox consortia to organic carbon amendments while simultaneously measuring anammox rates and tracking carbon flow into biomass, thus challenging a fundamental physiological assumption in their environmental niche.

proposals.title: Quantifying the interplay of sulfide, reactive iron, and organic matter in controlling anaerobic ammonium oxidation (anammox) rates in coastal sediments
proposals.motivation: Anammox is a key nitrogen loss pathway in marine sediments, but its environmental controls, particularly its interaction with dominant sediment biogeochemical cycles like sulfate and iron reduction, remain poorly quantified. Understanding these controls is critical for predicting nitrogen budgets and their sensitivity to organic matter loading and redox changes in coastal systems, which are hotspots for nutrient processing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that anammox rates in coastal sediments are non-linearly regulated by the availability of sulfide (a potential inhibitor) and reactive iron phases (which can mitigate sulfide toxicity), with organic matter quality acting as a master variable controlling the redox zonation and the competitive balance between dissimilatory nitrate reduction to ammonium (DNRA) and denitrification.
proposals.method: We will conduct slurry and intact core incubations with sediments from Aarhus Bay, using 15N-labeled nitrate and ammonium to directly measure potential and in-situ anammox rates. Treatments will manipulate sulfide concentrations, reactive iron (III) oxide (ferrihydrite) availability, and organic matter quality (defined by C:N ratio and 13C NMR characterization). Rate measurements will be coupled with quantification of functional gene abundances (hzsB for anammox, nrfA for DNRA, nirS for denitrification) and porewater geochemistry (NH4+, NO2-, NO3-, Fe2+, HS-) to establish mechanistic links.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for predicting anammox activity based on measurable sediment properties (Fe(III):HS- ratio, organic reactivity). It will clarify the competitive niche of anammox bacteria within the complex network of carbon, sulfur, and iron cycling, directly contributing to more accurate models of benthic nitrogen removal.
proposals.novelty: While anammox is known to be sensitive to sulfide, this study uniquely integrates the modulating role of reactive iron and organic matter reactivity—core themes of Jørgensen's research—to explain the spatial distribution and rates of anammox in dynamic coastal sediments, moving beyond simple correlation to a process-based understanding.

proposals.title: Constraining in situ anammox rates in marine sediments through bioreactor-calibrated metabolic flux analysis of porewater metabolite profiles
proposals.motivation: Anaerobic ammonium oxidation (anammox) is a key nitrogen loss pathway in marine sediments, but its in situ rates are notoriously difficult to quantify, especially in deep, energy-limited subsurface environments. Current methods, like isotope tracing, are challenging to apply at depth and may not capture the integrated activity over time. A novel approach is needed to infer rates from the chemical fingerprints of metabolism that are preserved in porewater profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the stoichiometric relationships between ammonium, nitrite, and nitrate depletion, calibrated under controlled bioreactor conditions, can be used to construct a metabolic network model that accurately infers in situ anammox rates from environmental porewater concentration gradients.
proposals.method: We will first cultivate marine anammox bacteria in continuous bioreactors under energy-limitation regimes mimicking subsurface conditions, precisely measuring substrate consumption and product formation to define a core metabolic flux model. This model will be implemented using constraint-based flux balance analysis (FBA) with the COBRA toolbox. We will then apply this calibrated model to high-resolution porewater profiles (NH4+, NO2-, NO3-) from sediment cores, using a reactive-transport inverse modeling framework to solve for the in situ anammox flux that best explains the observed chemical gradients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, geochemically-grounded method for estimating anammox rates in sediments where direct rate measurements are impractical. It will advance our quantitative understanding of the nitrogen cycle in the deep biosphere and help reconcile discrepancies between rate measurements and geochemical budgets. The framework can be extended to other key anaerobic processes like sulfate and iron reduction.
proposals.novelty: While metabolic modeling is established in biotechnology, its application to infer in situ biogeochemical rates from environmental metabolite data is novel. This bridges the gap between controlled laboratory physiology and the complex, low-energy state of the deep subseafloor biosphere, a core research theme of Jørgensen's group.

proposals.title: Linking Anammox Activity to Community Structure and Carbon Metabolism in Marine Sediments via DNA/RNA-SIP
proposals.motivation: Anaerobic ammonium oxidation (anammox) is a key nitrogen loss pathway in marine sediments, but its quantitative importance relative to denitrification and its coupling to organic carbon cycling remains poorly constrained in many environments. Understanding the active microbial community and its metabolic interactions is crucial for modeling nitrogen budgets and carbon oxidation pathways, particularly in sediments where sulfate reduction and iron cycling are dominant.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that anammox activity in continental margin sediments is directly fueled by specific, low-molecular-weight organic compounds or fermentation products, and that DNA/RNA-SIP will reveal a core consortium of active anammox bacteria (e.g., 'Candidatus Scalindua') tightly associated with fermentative and syntrophic partners.
proposals.method: We will conduct slurry incubations with anoxic sediments from Aarhus Bay or other well-characterized sites, amending with 15N-labeled ammonium and 13C-labeled potential organic substrates (e.g., acetate, formate, pyruvate). After incubation, nucleic acids will be extracted and subjected to isopycnic centrifugation for SIP. Heavy (13C/15N-labeled) and light fractions will be analyzed via 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing and metatranscriptomics to identify active, substrate-assimilating community members. Anammox rates will be quantified via 15N2 production, and process rates (sulfate reduction, iron reduction) will be measured using established 35S and 55Fe radiotracer techniques to contextualize the microbial activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link the identity of active anammox organisms and their associated community to specific carbon substrates in a pre-2026 methodological framework. It will provide quantitative data on the contribution of anammox to total N-loss and its relationship to other major electron-accepting processes (sulfate, iron reduction), refining global biogeochemical models.
proposals.novelty: While SIP has been used in other systems, its targeted application to identify the carbon metabolism of active anammox communities in complex sediments, integrating rate measurements of multiple concurrent biogeochemical processes, is a novel synthesis of Jørgensen's expertise in sediment biogeochemistry with modern molecular tools.

proposals.title: Quantifying Past Nitrogen Cycle Dynamics: An Inverse Modeling Approach to Constrain Anammox Rates and Water Column Redox from Sedimentary Ladderane Lipid Accumulation
proposals.motivation: Ladderane lipids are unique biomarkers for anaerobic ammonium oxidation (anammox), a key process in the marine nitrogen cycle that removes fixed nitrogen. While their sedimentary presence qualitatively indicates past anammox activity, quantitative reconstruction of in-situ rates and overlying water column redox conditions remains a major challenge. This limits our ability to model past oceanic nutrient budgets and their feedbacks on climate and productivity.
proposals.hypothesis: Sedimentary ladderane lipid concentrations and isomer patterns can be used, via an inverse modeling framework, to quantitatively estimate past anammox reaction rates and constrain the depth and intensity of the oxygen minimum zone (OMZ) in the overlying water column.
proposals.method: We will develop a reactive-transport model (RTM) that couples the diagenesis of organic matter, nitrogen species (NH4+, NO2-, NO3-), and ladderane lipids in marine sediments. The forward model will simulate ladderane production (linked to anammox rate), degradation, and burial based on pre-2026 kinetic parameters. We will then formulate and solve the inverse problem using a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm to find the set of past boundary conditions (e.g., bottom-water O2, NOx fluxes) and anammox rate constants that best fit observed down-core ladderane profiles from well-characterized OMZ sediments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework to translate sedimentary biomarker records into estimates of past nitrogen transformation fluxes. It will advance the use of molecular fossils beyond qualitative indicators to tools for rate quantification, directly linking to Jørgensen's research on microbial metabolic rates in sediments. The method will offer new constraints for paleoceanographic models of nutrient cycling.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying formal inverse problem methodology, common in geophysics and modern rate studies, to the quantitative interpretation of lipid biomarker stratigraphy. It moves beyond correlation to mechanistically derive rates and water column properties from sedimentary archives.

ideas.title: Adapting Eddy Covariance for In Situ Quantification of AOM-Driven Carbonate Precipitation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the eddy covariance technique from atmospheric science to quantify anaerobic oxidation of methane (AOM)-driven carbonate precipitation rates in situ. The analogous problem—measuring net gas exchange between a complex surface and the fluid above it without disturbance—is solved by calculating the covariance between high-frequency vertical velocity and scalar concentration measurements. Here, we propose deploying a micro-sensor array within the benthic boundary layer over microbial mats to measure high-frequency fluid flow and the concentrations of key ions involved in carbonate precipitation (e.g., Ca2+, HCO3-, pH). The calculated covariance provides a direct flux of these ions into or out of the mat, which is directly linked to the rate of carbonate mineral formation. This method would yield an integrated, non-destructive measure of precipitation rate and efficiency under fully natural hydrodynamic and geochemical conditions, overcoming the limitations of lab incubations or ex situ mineral analyses.

ideas.description: This research integrates dense, in situ microsensor measurements of chemical gradients (O2, sulfide, pH, CH4) with geostatistical kriging techniques to model and predict the spatial distribution of microbial functional groups within complex mats or sediments. By treating the chemical parameters as primary regionalized variables, the method constructs a predictive map of the geochemical landscape. This chemical landscape model then serves as a covariate in a co-kriging framework to interpolate microbial community composition between physical sampling points, revealing how chemical microenvironments control microbial biogeography at sub-millimeter scales.

ideas.title: Functional Gradients at the Simulated Hydrate Interface: Defining the Interface Community by Activity and Position
ideas.description: This study abandons the destructive sampling of a natural single crystal in favor of a biomimetic microfluidic platform that replicates the essential physical-chemical gradients of the methane hydrate-microbe interface. The system uses a defined porous matrix to create a stable diffusive boundary layer for methane and other solutes, allowing microbial colonization from environmental inocula. The true 'interface community' is then defined not by physical dissection but by its metabolic activity and precise location within this gradient, measured via in situ chemical imaging, stable isotope probing, and spatially resolved transcriptomics. This approach enables the first causal, time-resolved analysis of how gradient dynamics structure the community and its functions at this critical boundary.

ideas.title: Dynamic Trait-Based Microbial Functional Modules for Probabilistic Projection of Methane and Carbon Fluxes
ideas.description: This research develops and integrates dynamic, trait-based microbial functional modules into global biogeochemical models to project methane cycling and carbon sequestration. Instead of static distribution maps, the modules use genomic trait databases and environmental drivers to simulate microbial community functional responses and process rates probabilistically. The system is rigorously calibrated and validated against data from cross-scale manipulative experiments (e.g., warming, drying) and global observatory time-series. This approach directly quantifies and reduces uncertainty in model projections by mechanistically linking microbial ecology to ecosystem function under environmental change.

ideas.title: Spatially resolved chemical gradient mapping at the single methane hydrate crystal-microbe interface
ideas.description: This research direction integrates fine-scale microsensor technology directly into the study of the single methane hydrate crystal-microbe interface. By deploying O2, sulfide, pH, and methane microsensors with micron-scale spatial resolution, we can map the dynamic chemical gradients that radiate from the crystal surface through the attached biofilm. This allows us to quantify the rates of methane release from the crystal and its immediate consumption or conversion by the interfacial microbial community. The synergy creates a closed-loop understanding, directly correlating specific microbial functions and spatial organization at the interface with the chemical fluxes they generate.

ideas.title: Track lipid synthesis, exchange, and modification dynamics in ANME-1/SRB consortia using time-resolved isotope probing and multi-omics
ideas.description: This redesigned study moves beyond a static lipid inventory to resolve the dynamic lipidome of anaerobic methane-oxidizing consortia. Using continuous-flow bioreactors with 13C-labeled substrates, it applies lipid-SIP coupled with metatranscriptomics and proteomics across a time series to capture the synthesis and fate of lipids. This approach directly identifies which lipids are uniquely produced by ANME-1 or its SRB partner, which are exchanged or co-modified, and links specific lipid structures to the expression of key biosynthetic and modification genes during active methane oxidation.

ideas.title: Challenging the Sulfate Monopoly: A Search for Alternative Electron Acceptors in AOM via an Electrometallurgy Analogy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption that sulfate reduction is the sole electron sink for anaerobic oxidation of methane (AOM) in microbial mats. By drawing an analogy from industrial electrometallurgy and corrosion science—where manipulating electrochemical potentials unlocks alternative, non-obvious cathodic reactions to drive anodic processes—we propose a systematic search for alternative electron acceptors. The adapted research will treat the AOM consortium as a biogeochemical battery, using electrochemical techniques to probe whether alternative oxidized species (e.g., ferric iron, humics, or trace metals) can serve as viable electron acceptors, thereby expanding the known thermodynamic and ecological niches for methane oxidation in anoxic environments.

ideas.title: Resolving geochemical drivers of AOM consortium structure, function, and resilience through integrated multi-omics
ideas.description: This study employs chemostat-based mat incubations under dynamically controlled gradients of sulfate, methane, and other ions to simulate environmental shifts. Integrated time-series metagenomics and metatranscriptomics track community composition and gene expression, while 13C-stable isotope probing identifies active autotrophs and quantifies carbon fixation. This multi-omics framework directly tests whether changing geochemistry modulates the activity of a stable consortium or triggers a successional restructuring, providing a mechanistic basis for predicting mat and reef stability under natural perturbation.

ideas.title: Disentangling causal drivers: A global sampling design to isolate geochemical from geographic effects on subseafloor microbiomes
ideas.description: This study implements a strategically paired sampling design to break the inherent correlation between geography and geochemistry in the seabed. By comparing communities from geographically distant yet geochemically similar environments, and vice-versa, we can isolate their individual effects. We will apply causal modeling statistics to metagenomic and geochemical data from these targeted sites to quantify the direct causal influence of geochemical parameters versus the indirect influence mediated through geographical separation. This approach moves beyond correlation to robustly test if geochemistry is a primary driver that can override the historical and dispersal constraints of geography.

ideas.title: A High-Throughput Microbioreactor Platform for Mapping AOM Consortia Fitness Landscapes
ideas.description: This idea adapts high-throughput screening methods from enzyme engineering to study anaerobic oxidation of methane (AOM) consortia. Just as protein engineers test enzyme variants across a matrix of conditions to map fitness landscapes, we propose culturing AOM mats in automated microbioreactor arrays. Each reactor in the array will be subjected to a precisely controlled, unique combination of geochemical variables—including sulfate concentration, methane partial pressure, levels of key ions (e.g., ammonium, iron), and pH. Using in-situ optical and electrochemical sensors to continuously monitor metabolic outputs (e.g., sulfide production, gas consumption), the experiment will generate a high-resolution dataset. This will map the consortia's metabolic 'fitness' across a multidimensional geochemical landscape, identifying optimal zones for activity, inhibitory thresholds, and complex parameter interactions that control reef growth, thereby moving beyond one-factor-at-a-time experiments.

ideas.title: Employ sequential, multiplexed FISH with spectral barcoding for high-resolution spatial mapping of microbial consortia in sediment cores
ideas.description: This idea adapts sequential, multiplexed fluorescence in situ hybridization (seqFISH/multiplexed FISH) from the field of developmental biology and neuroscience, where it is used to map the spatial organization of numerous cell types in complex tissues like the brain. The analogous solution involves using multiple rounds of hybridization with spectrally distinct fluorescent probes to create unique optical barcodes for many different targets within an intact sample. Adapted to microbial ecology, this method would be applied to sediment core sections to visualize and map the physical arrangement and co-localization of not just a few dominant groups, but dozens of key microbial taxa (e.g., sulfate-reducers, methanogens, fermenters) simultaneously. By designing probe sets for their ribosomal RNA and performing sequential hybridization cycles, each taxonomic group receives a unique spectral barcode, enabling the creation of a high-resolution, multi-taxa spatial map of microbial consortia and their micro-niches within the sedimentary architecture.

ideas.title: Decoupling AOM from physical association using multi-barrier continuous-flow reactors with high-resolution activity tracking
ideas.description: This redesigned experiment tests for anaerobic oxidation of methane via truly dispersed cells or soluble intermediates by physically and continuously separating microbial partners or metabolites. A continuous-flow bioreactor containing a known AOM consortium is subjected to serial, progressively finer membrane separations, with each stage's effluent monitored in real-time for methane oxidation activity using 13C-CH4 tracer. High-sensitivity, inline cell imaging and chemical analysis of the effluent definitively confirm the absence of cell aggregates or particles, while tracking the fate of carbon into potential soluble intermediates and biomass. This design overcomes the flaw by providing continuous, dynamic proof of separation, preventing the re-aggregation or cryptic association possible in batch setups.

ideas.title: Correlative Confocal 3D-FISH for High-Resolution Spatial-Ecological Mapping of Microbial Consortia
ideas.description: This fused approach integrates confocal microscopy and fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) into a single, correlative workflow applied to minimally disturbed environmental samples like microbial mats or sediment cores. First, 3D confocal imaging with general fluorescent probes (e.g., for EPS, lipids, or autofluorescence) captures the detailed physical architecture and microenvironment of the consortium. Subsequently, the same physical sample section is subjected to multi-channel FISH using phylogenetic probes targeting the dominant archaeal and bacterial groups. By precisely overlaying the 3D architectural map with the 3D phylogenetic map, the method reveals not just who is next to whom, but how specific microbial cells and clusters are positioned within the consortium's structural framework, providing direct visual evidence for putative syntrophic partnerships and niche partitioning.

ideas.title: Non-invasive planar optode imaging for high-resolution 2D chemical mapping of intact microbial mats
ideas.description: This redesigned system uses a sheet of optical sensor foil (planar optode) containing multiple luminescent indicator spots sensitive to O2, pH, and sulfide. The foil is placed in gentle contact with the surface of an intact microbial mat core or in-situ mat. A controlled imaging setup with specific excitation wavelengths and a scientific camera captures the luminescence lifetime or intensity, which is calibrated to chemical concentrations. This generates real-time, high-resolution 2D maps of chemical gradients across the mat surface without any physical probes disrupting the internal mat architecture, providing a true snapshot of its biogeochemical zonation.

ideas.title: Correlative super-resolution and electron microscopy to resolve nanoscale consortium architecture and physical interfaces
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the resolution barrier of confocal microscopy by first using single-molecule localization microscopy (SMLM) techniques to map the nanoscale distribution of archaeal and bacterial taxa within the mat. Following this fluorescent nanoscale mapping, the same sample regions are processed for volume electron microscopy, specifically FIB-SEM, to acquire 3D ultrastructural data. The correlative dataset precisely aligns the taxonomic identity from super-resolved fluorescence with the physical context from electron microscopy, revealing direct interfaces, putative exchange structures, and EPS organization. This integrated method provides a functional map linking microbial identity to the physical geometry of interaction at the relevant nanometer scale.

ideas.title: Adapting Microfluidic Single-Cell Trapping for Single Hydrate Crystal-Microbe Interface Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from microbial single-cell biophysics to investigate the microbial community and processes at the direct interface of a single methane hydrate crystal. The analogous problem—studying individual microbial interactions with specific surfaces—was solved using microfluidic devices with precision traps to isolate single cells against defined substrates. By designing a microfluidic 'crystal trap' to immobilize a single hydrate crystal and then hydrodynamically guide and trap individual microbial cells or defined consortia onto a specific crystal face, we create a controlled, isolated interface. This platform enables direct, high-resolution investigation of attachment dynamics, metabolic activity, and physical interactions (via force spectroscopy) at this fundamental scale, followed by targeted single-cell omics analysis of the interface-specific microbes.

ideas.title: Activity-targeted single-cell sorting and hybrid sequencing for high-fidelity genomes of uncultivated DSAG and JS1
ideas.description: This redesigned approach first uses substrate-based activity probes (e.g., BONCAT with click chemistry) to fluorescently label metabolically active DSAG and JS1 cells in environmental samples, enabling high-precision sorting. Sorted active cells are processed using ultra-low-input, enzyme-based amplification-free library preparation to minimize bias and chimerism. The resulting single-cell genomic data is then integrated with deep metagenomic sequencing from the same sample, using co-assembly and binning to scaffold fragmented single-cell assemblies, validate completeness, and correct for amplification artifacts, thereby producing robust, near-complete genomes that accurately reflect in situ metabolic function.

ideas.title: Hybrid Machine Learning-Geostatistical Framework for Predicting Microbial Distributions
ideas.description: This approach combines machine learning and geostatistics to overcome the non-stationarity of microbial spatial data. First, a machine learning model (e.g., Random Forest) uses environmental covariates to predict the large-scale, deterministic component of microbial community composition. The spatial autocorrelation structure is then modeled from the residuals of this prediction, which are more likely to be stationary. Finally, regression kriging is performed, adding the kriged residuals back to the machine learning prediction to generate a final, robust interpolation that respects both environmental drivers and local spatial structure.

ideas.title: Adapting Neural Probe Arrays to Map Chemical Gradients in Microbial Mats
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from neuroscience to solve the problem of mapping fine-scale chemical gradients in microbial mats. In neurophysiology, high-density microelectrode arrays (like Neuropixels probes) are used to penetrate brain tissue and record spatial-temporal electrical activity from hundreds of points simultaneously. We adapt this by designing a 'Chemical Neuropixels' array—a thin, penetrating shank containing a dense grid of solid-state chemical microsensors for O2, sulfide, pH, and methane. This device would be inserted into a microbial mat core, enabling the simultaneous capture of high-resolution, multi-parameter chemical gradients across both depth and lateral dimensions in a single, minimally disruptive deployment, providing a dynamic, multi-dimensional chemical map of the mat's internal microenvironment.
